proposals.title: Dissecting the MHC-I-Independent Mechanisms of Interferon-Alpha in Suppressing Metastasis and Modulating the Tumor Microenvironment
proposals.motivation: Interferon-alpha (IFN-α) has demonstrated anti-metastatic and anti-angiogenic activity in preclinical models, including Dr. Fidler's work on colon cancer liver metastasis. The canonical mechanism involves upregulation of Major Histocompatibility Complex class I (MHC-I) on tumor cells, enhancing immune recognition. However, this assumption may oversimplify IFN-α's pleiotropic effects, potentially overlooking critical direct effects on endothelial cells, stromal cells, and non-canonical signaling pathways that are independent of antigen presentation and crucial for its therapeutic efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of IFN-α's anti-metastatic and anti-angiogenic activity is mediated through mechanisms primary to and independent of its effect on tumor cell MHC-I expression, involving direct modulation of the tumor vasculature and microenvironment.
proposals.method: We will use a syngeneic murine model of renal cell carcinoma (RENCA) metastasis. Tumor cells will be engineered using CRISPR-Cas9 (pre-2026 technology) to knockout B2m, ablating surface MHC-I expression. Metastatic burden and angiogenesis will be quantified in control and B2m-KO tumors following systemic IFN-α therapy. We will perform bulk RNA sequencing on sorted tumor-associated endothelial cells and intratumoral leukocytes from these cohorts. Differential expression and pathway analysis (using algorithms like DESeq2 and GSEA) will identify MHC-I-independent IFN-response signatures. Key findings will be validated in human endothelial cell cultures stimulated with IFN-α.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the relative contribution of MHC-I-dependent versus independent pathways to IFN-α's anti-metastatic effect. It will identify novel IFN-responsive genes and pathways in the tumor vasculature that correlate with therapy outcome, providing a refined mechanistic framework for interferon-based therapies and potential biomarkers for patient stratification.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the prevailing paradigm that IFN-α's anti-metastatic effect is primarily immunogenic via MHC-I upregulation. It innovatively combines genetic ablation of MHC-I with state-of-the-art transcriptomic profiling of specific tumor microenvironment components to isolate non-canonical mechanisms.

proposals.title: Systemic Screening of Cytokine-Induced Transient Modulation of Metastatic Gene Expression in Human Carcinoma Cells
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Fidler and others established that systemic interferon-alpha (IFN-α) can suppress angiogenesis and metastasis, while IFN-γ is known to modulate immunogenicity and tumor cell phenotype. This suggests cytokines can induce transient, therapeutically exploitable changes in metastatic potential. However, a systematic investigation of other clinically relevant cytokines beyond interferons is lacking, representing a significant gap in understanding the pleiotropic effects of cytokine signaling on metastasis-regulatory pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific cytokines, including interleukins (e.g., IL-6, IL-10, IL-12) and colony-stimulating factors (e.g., G-CSF, GM-CSF), can induce transient, reversible changes in the expression of genes regulating metastasis and angiogenesis in human tumor cells, thereby altering their metastatic potential in vivo.
proposals.method: We will treat a panel of established human renal cell carcinoma and colon carcinoma lines (pre-2026 models from Fidler's lab) with a library of 20+ recombinant human cytokines. Using pre-2026 standard methods, we will perform time-course analyses (0, 6, 24, 48h) via quantitative RT-PCR and multiplex ELISA to measure mRNA and protein expression of key metastasis/angiogenesis regulators (e.g., VEGF, MMP-9, E-cadherin, CXCR4). Functional validation will employ short-term (24h) cytokine pre-treatment followed by standard in vitro assays for invasion (Matrigel) and endothelial tube disruption, and in vivo assays for experimental liver metastasis in nude mice.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a first-of-its-kind map linking specific cytokine exposures to transient phenotypic shifts in metastatic competence. It will identify candidate cytokines for adjuvant therapy to suppress metastasis and may reveal cytokines that inadvertently promote metastatic traits, informing safer use of immunomodulatory agents. The findings will directly extend Fidler's seminal work on interferon-based anti-metastatic therapy.
proposals.novelty: While the anti-metastatic effects of IFNs are documented, a broad, systematic screen for transient modulatory effects by other cytokine families has not been conducted. The focus on transient, potentially reversible modulation of metastatic potential, rather than permanent cytotoxic effects, represents a novel therapeutic angle grounded in Fidler's 'seed and soil' paradigm.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Role of IFN-γ in Modulating Tumor Cell Adhesion Molecules and Cytoskeletal Dynamics to Promote Pulmonary Capillary Retention
proposals.motivation: The organ-specific metastasis of cancer cells is a critical determinant of patient mortality. While IFN-γ is known to have anti-tumor and anti-angiogenic effects, pre-2026 clinical and experimental observations suggest it may paradoxically influence metastatic patterns by altering tumor cell interactions with the microvasculature. Understanding this mechanism is vital for developing therapies that block metastasis without compromising immune surveillance.
proposals.hypothesis: Brief exposure of tumor cells to IFN-γ induces the transcriptional upregulation of specific adhesion molecules (e.g., ICAM-1, VCAM-1) and cytoskeletal regulators (e.g., Rho GTPases), which collectively enhance tumor cell stiffness and adhesion to pulmonary capillary endothelial cells, leading to increased mechanical retention.
proposals.method: We will use human carcinoma cell lines (e.g., colon, renal) and treat them with a brief, physiologically relevant pulse of IFN-γ. Molecular changes will be profiled via RNA-seq and validated by qPCR and western blot for adhesion and cytoskeletal genes. Functional assays will include static and flow-based adhesion to human pulmonary microvascular endothelial cell (HPMEC) monolayers, measurement of cell stiffness via atomic force microscopy, and in vivo quantification of pulmonary retention using bioluminescent imaging in murine models. Data integration will employ pathway analysis (e.g., GSEA) and correlation with adhesion/retention metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a specific molecular program activated by IFN-γ that directly facilitates the early, rate-limiting step of metastatic seeding in the lung. It will provide a novel link between cytokine signaling, tumor cell biomechanics, and organotropism, offering potential targets (e.g., specific adhesion pairs) for anti-metastatic therapy.
proposals.novelty: While IFN-γ's immunomodulatory roles are well-studied, its direct, rapid effect on tumor cell physical properties and adhesion to organ-specific endothelium is a novel and non-obvious mechanism for influencing metastatic organotropism, moving beyond its canonical anti-angiogenic or immunogenic cell death functions.

proposals.title: Early Transcriptomic Profiling Identifies a Cytokine-Induced Retention Signature Predictive of Metastatic Dormancy in Human Carcinoma Cells
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Fidler's own, demonstrates that cytokines like interferon-alpha can suppress metastasis, but the mechanisms remain incompletely defined. A critical gap is understanding the earliest, potentially reversible, transcriptional changes in tumor cells that may commit them to a non-proliferative, organ-retained state, as opposed to overt metastatic outgrowth. Identifying this 'retention signature' could provide novel biomarkers for therapeutic response and new targets to induce metastatic dormancy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a brief, sub-lethal exposure of human carcinoma cells to metastasis-suppressive cytokines (e.g., IFN-α) induces a specific and conserved early gene expression signature that correlates with in vivo metastatic retention and dormancy, and that this signature can be distinguished from expression changes associated with cell death or stasis.
proposals.method: Human renal cell carcinoma and colon carcinoma lines will be treated in vitro with IFN-α or vehicle for 6-24 hours. RNA will be extracted and subjected to bulk RNA-sequencing (Illumina platform). Differential expression analysis will be performed using DESeq2 and edgeR. The resulting gene lists will be refined via gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) and weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA) to identify core modules. The predictive power of the derived signature will be validated against existing patient transcriptomic datasets (e.g., TCGA) annotated with metastatic progression data.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel, early transcriptional signature associated with cytokine-induced metastatic suppression, moving beyond descriptive phenomenology to a mechanistic predictor. It will provide a set of candidate genes whose expression can be monitored as early biomarkers of therapeutic efficacy in adjuvant settings. Furthermore, it will establish a functional genomics framework for identifying the master regulators of metastatic dormancy.
proposals.novelty: While transcriptomics of established metastases or long-term treated cells is common, applying high-resolution profiling to the earliest timepoints after cytokine exposure to decipher a 'fate-commitment' signature is novel. This approach leverages Dr. Fidler's foundational work on the organ-specificity of metastasis and cytokine therapy to ask a new, mechanism-oriented question about the initial molecular decision points.

proposals.title: Pharmacological Reversal of IFN-γ-Induced Metastatic Competence in Human Carcinoma Cells: Targeting the JAK/STAT-PD-L1 Axis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that transient exposure to interferon-gamma (IFN-γ), often from anti-tumor immune responses, can paradoxically enhance the metastatic potential of surviving tumor cells by inducing an epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT)-like state and upregulating immune checkpoint molecules like PD-L1. This creates a critical therapeutic gap: while IFN-γ has anti-proliferative effects, its pro-metastatic sequelae may undermine long-term patient survival. Blocking this adverse effect could improve outcomes by preventing therapy-induced metastatic spread.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, timed pharmacological inhibition of the JAK/STAT signaling pathway activated by transient IFN-γ exposure will prevent the subsequent acquisition of metastatic traits in human colon and renal carcinoma cells without abrogating the cytokine's direct anti-proliferative effects.
proposals.method: Human colon (HT-29) and renal (786-O) carcinoma cell lines will be treated with a short, clinically relevant pulse of IFN-γ. Using pre-2026 methods, we will employ RNA-seq to define the transient transcriptomic signature of 'metastatic competence,' focusing on EMT regulators (e.g., SNAIL, TWIST) and PD-L1. High-throughput screening of a library of FDA-approved kinase inhibitors and JAK/STAT pathway antagonists (e.g., ruxolitinib) will be conducted to identify agents that block this signature. Efficacy will be validated in vitro using transwell migration/invasion assays and in vivo using the chick embryo chorioallantoic membrane (CAM) model of spontaneous metastasis from a primary tumor fragment.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify candidate pharmacological agents capable of decoupling the pro-metastatic from the anti-tumor effects of IFN-γ. It will provide a mechanistic framework for adjuvant therapy aimed at preventing metastasis driven by inflammatory tumor microenvironments. The findings could directly inform the design of combination therapies for cancers like RCC and colon cancer metastasis.
proposals.novelty: While IFN-γ's role in immunoediting is known, systematically targeting its transient signaling to specifically prevent metastasis is an underexplored 'inverse problem.' The approach is novel in its focus on temporal pharmacological intervention post-cytokine exposure, rather than on blocking IFN-γ production or receptor binding entirely.

proposals.title: Systemic IFN-γ Therapy for Cancer and its Paradoxical Effect on Circulating Tumor Cell Survival and Extravasation: A Mechanistic Preclinical Study
proposals.motivation: Interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) is a potent immunomodulatory cytokine with established anti-tumor and anti-angiogenic properties, leading to its clinical investigation for cancer therapy. However, pre-2026 research, including Dr. Fidler's own work on the 'seed and soil' hypothesis, demonstrates that cytokines can have complex, context-dependent effects on tumor and endothelial cells. A critical, unresolved question is whether the systemic administration of IFN-γ, intended to activate immunity, could inadvertently enhance the metastatic potential of surviving circulating tumor cells (CTCs) by altering endothelial cell adhesion and vascular permeability, thereby promoting metastatic seeding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systemic exposure to IFN-γ, at concentrations achievable during clinical therapy, will upregulate specific endothelial cell adhesion molecules (e.g., ICAM-1, VCAM-1) and alter endothelial barrier function, thereby increasing the arrest and transendothelial migration of CTCs, ultimately facilitating metastatic colonization in secondary organs.
proposals.method: We will employ human endothelial cell (HUVEC) monolayers in vitro, exposed to clinically relevant doses of IFN-γ (1-100 ng/mL). Endothelial activation will be quantified via qRT-PCR and flow cytometry for adhesion molecule expression (ICAM-1, VCAM-1). Functional assays will use fluorescently labeled human carcinoma cells (e.g., PC-3 prostate, HT-29 colon) to measure adhesion under shear stress and transendothelial migration. In vivo, we will use a refined mouse model of hematogenous metastasis: luciferase-tagged tumor cells will be injected intravenously into mice pre-treated with systemic murine IFN-γ. Metastatic burden will be tracked longitudinally by bioluminescence imaging and quantified ex vivo. RNA-seq of lung endothelial cells from treated mice will identify IFN-γ-regulated pathways facilitating metastasis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental evidence linking clinical-grade IFN-γ exposure to pro-metastatic endothelial activation. It will define specific molecular mediators of this paradoxical effect, offering potential biomarkers (e.g., soluble ICAM-1) for patient monitoring. The findings will critically inform the risk-benefit assessment of cytokine-based immunotherapies and could lead to combinatorial strategies to block this unintended side effect.
proposals.novelty: While IFN-γ's anti-angiogenic effects are well-documented, its potential to directly promote the critical step of CTC extravasation via endothelial reprogramming is a novel and counterintuitive mechanism. This shifts the focus from IFN-γ's impact on the primary tumor or immune cells to its direct effect on the vascular 'soil' encountered by disseminating seeds.

proposals.title: Systemic Cytokine Pretreatment Modulates Organ-Specific Retention of Circulating Tumor Cells: A Study Beyond the Pulmonary Capillary Bed
proposals.motivation: The metastatic cascade is critically dependent on the arrest and extravasation of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in distant organ capillary beds. While the 'seed and soil' hypothesis is established, the mechanistic regulation of the initial 'trapping' step by host factors remains poorly understood, particularly for organs beyond the lungs. Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Fidler's own studies on interferon-alpha, shows systemic cytokines can alter the metastatic microenvironment, but their direct impact on CTC retention in specific organs is not defined.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pretreatment with specific cytokines (e.g., IFN-α, TNF-α) will differentially alter the expression of adhesion molecules on organ-specific capillary endothelial cells, thereby modulating the retention patterns of human tumor cells in the liver, bone marrow, and brain, independent of their effects on the lungs.
proposals.method: We will employ a well-established in vivo model of hematogenous metastasis. Nude mice will be pretreated systemically with IFN-α, TNF-α, or vehicle. Human tumor cells (colon carcinoma, renal cell carcinoma) will be fluorescently labeled (e.g., with CellTracker dyes) and injected intravenously. At early time points (5 min to 2 hours), mice will be sacrificed, and organs (lungs, liver, bone, brain) perfused. Quantitative analysis of retained fluorescent cells in tissue sections will be performed using automated fluorescence microscopy and image analysis software (e.g., ImageJ with custom macros for cell counting). Parallel in vitro assays will assess cytokine-induced changes in adhesion molecule (ICAM-1, VCAM-1, E-selectin) expression on human endothelial cells from different organ microvascular beds using flow cytometry and qRT-PCR.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly link systemic cytokine exposure to the organotropism of metastasis by defining its effect on the initial physical retention of CTCs. It will provide a mechanistic basis for the anti-metastatic effects of cytokines like IFN-α beyond angiogenesis suppression. The findings could inform the use of cytokine therapies to specifically alter the metastatic destination of disseminated cells.
proposals.novelty: While cytokine effects on endothelium and later metastatic steps are known, systematically quantifying their direct, immediate impact on tumor cell retention across multiple organ capillary beds in vivo is a novel and underexplored approach. It shifts the focus from the final metastatic colony to the critical, earlier event of capillary arrest.

proposals.title: Genetic Determinants of IL-8 Expression in Ovarian Carcinoma: A Cell-Line Specific Analysis of Angiogenic Phenotype
proposals.motivation: Interleukin-8 (IL-8) is a potent pro-angiogenic and pro-metastatic chemokine implicated in tumor progression. However, its regulation in cancer appears heterogeneous, with studies in other cancers suggesting induction may depend on specific oncogenic drivers. Ovarian cancer is a highly lethal malignancy where ascites formation and peritoneal metastasis are driven by angiogenesis, yet the genetic factors controlling key angiogenic signals like IL-8 in ovarian tumors remain poorly defined. Testing the generality of IL-8 induction across diverse genetic backgrounds is critical for understanding patient-specific tumor microenvironments and designing targeted anti-angiogenic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the induction and expression level of IL-8 in ovarian cancer cells is not a universal feature but is instead determined by specific genetic alterations, such as mutations in TP53, BRCA1/2, or components of the PI3K pathway, which define major molecular subtypes of the disease.
proposals.method: We will utilize a panel of 10-12 well-characterized human ovarian cancer cell lines (e.g., OVCAR-3, SKOV-3, A2780, HeyA8) representing distinct genetic backgrounds (TP53 status, BRCAness, KRAS/BRAF mutations). Cells will be cultured under standard and hypoxic (1% O2) conditions to mimic the tumor microenvironment. IL-8 mRNA expression will be quantified via RT-qPCR using TaqMan assays, and secreted IL-8 protein will be measured by ELISA. Correlation analysis will be performed between baseline/hypoxia-induced IL-8 levels and the cell lines' documented mutational profiles and pathway activation status (e.g., phospho-AKT, NF-κB). Functional validation will involve siRNA-mediated knockdown of candidate regulators (e.g., mutant p53, PTEN) in high-IL-8 expressors to confirm causality.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between the genetic landscape of ovarian cancer and the expression of a major angiogenic factor, IL-8. It will provide a mechanistic framework for predicting which patient tumors are likely to be IL-8-driven, thereby informing the selection of patients who might benefit from IL-8 pathway inhibition. The findings will refine the 'seed and soil' hypothesis for ovarian metastasis by defining how the genetic 'seed' influences the angiogenic 'soil'.
proposals.novelty: While IL-8's role in cancer is known, systematic interrogation of its genetic regulation across the molecular spectrum of ovarian cancer is lacking. This moves beyond descriptive correlation to test causality between specific driver mutations and a key phenotypic output, challenging the assumption that IL-8 is a ubiquitous response in all tumor cells.

proposals.title: Unbiased Identification of pH-Sensitive Transcription Factors Binding the IL-8 Promoter: A Novel Mechanism for Acidosis-Driven Angiogenesis in Cancer Metastasis
proposals.motivation: The acidic tumor microenvironment, a hallmark of solid tumors, drives metastasis by upregulating pro-angiogenic and pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-8. Pre-2026 dogma attributes this primarily to AP-1 and NF-κB activation, yet this may be incomplete, limiting therapeutic targeting. A systematic, unbiased discovery of transcription factors (TFs) whose DNA-binding is directly modulated by acidosis could reveal novel, targetable pathways controlling this critical tumor-host interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extracellular acidosis directly alters the DNA-binding affinity of transcription factors beyond AP-1/NF-κB for the IL-8 promoter, and that these pH-sensitive TFs are critical, previously unrecognized mediators of acidosis-induced angiogenesis and metastasis.
proposals.method: We will employ a systematic protein-DNA interaction screen adapted from plant abiotic stress research. A comprehensive library of human TF open reading frames (ORFs) will be screened against the IL-8 promoter using a high-throughput protein-binding microarray (PBM) platform under precisely controlled buffer conditions mimicking normal (pH 7.4) and acidic (pH 6.5-6.8) tumor microenvironments. Hits demonstrating a significant, reproducible pH-dependent binding shift will be validated via electrophoretic mobility shift assays (EMSAs) under identical conditions and their functional role confirmed by siRNA knockdown in human endothelial and carcinoma cells cultured at low pH, assessing IL-8 mRNA expression (via qRT-PCR) and angiogenic potential.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive map of TFs with pH-sensitive affinity for a key metastatic gene promoter. It will directly challenge the AP-1/NF-κB-centric model of acidosis signaling and may identify novel, druggable transcriptional regulators of the metastatic cascade. The findings could establish a new paradigm for understanding how solid tumors exploit microenvironmental acidosis to drive angiogenesis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying an unbiased, systematic screening approach from plant stress biology to the cancer acidosis problem, moving beyond candidate gene studies to directly test the long-held assumption that AP-1/NF-κB are the primary mediators, thereby opening a new avenue for discovery in tumor microenvironment research.

proposals.title: Defining the pH-Activated NF-κB and AP-1 Subunit Composition in Ovarian Cancer Using EMSA/Supershift Analysis
proposals.motivation: The acidic tumor microenvironment is a potent driver of cancer progression and metastasis, known to activate key transcription factors like NF-κB and AP-1, which regulate genes for cell survival, angiogenesis, and invasion. However, the precise dimeric composition of these factors activated by low pH remains undefined, creating a critical gap in understanding this fundamental pH-sensing pathway. This mechanistic ambiguity hinders the development of targeted strategies to disrupt pH-mediated oncogenic signaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acidic pH selectively activates specific NF-κB (e.g., canonical p50-p65 vs. non-canonical p52-RelB) and AP-1 (e.g., c-Jun/Fra-1 vs. JunB/FosB) dimeric complexes in ovarian cancer cells, and that these specific subunit compositions dictate a unique pro-tumorigenic gene expression profile.
proposals.method: We will culture human ovarian cancer cell lines under physiologically acidic (pH 6.5-6.8) and control (pH 7.4) conditions. Nuclear extracts will be prepared and subjected to classic Electrophoretic Mobility Shift Assay (EMSA) using radiolabeled consensus DNA probes for NF-κB and AP-1. For supershift analysis, extracts will be pre-incubated with a comprehensive panel of specific antibodies targeting individual NF-κB (p50, p52, p65, RelB, c-Rel) and AP-1 (c-Jun, JunB, JunD, c-Fos, FosB, Fra-1) subunits prior to EMSA. Antibody-bound complexes will be identified by a further retardation (supershift) or ablation of the EMSA band.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first definitive molecular map of the exact transcription factor subunits activated by tumor acidosis in ovarian cancer, moving beyond generic 'NF-κB/AP-1 activation' to a subunit-specific mechanism. It will establish a direct methodological link between an environmental cue (low pH) and specific dimeric transcription factors, offering precise molecular targets for future therapeutic intervention to block pH-driven malignancy.
proposals.novelty: While EMSA/supershift is a standard technique in immunology and virology, its direct application to deconvolute the specific transcription factor dimers activated by a physicochemical feature of the tumor microenvironment (acidic pH) is novel and represents a powerful, yet underutilized, methodological analogy for cancer biology.

proposals.title: Characterization of Acidic pH-Induced NF-kappaB and AP-1 Subunit Heterodimers in Ovarian Cancer Progression and Metastasis
proposals.motivation: The acidic tumor microenvironment, a hallmark of solid tumors like ovarian cancer, is a potent promoter of invasion, angiogenesis, and therapy resistance. Pre-2026 studies implicate the transcription factors NF-kappaB and AP-1 in pH-sensing, but their activation is pleiotropic, involving multiple subunit combinations (e.g., RelA/p50, c-Fos/c-Jun). The specific heterodimers activated by acidosis in ovarian cancer remain undefined, creating a critical gap in understanding how this environmental cue reprograms malignant gene expression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acidic pH selectively activates specific NF-kappaB (e.g., RelB/p52) and AP-1 (e.g., Fra-1/JunB) subunit heterodimers in ovarian cancer cells, and that these specific complexes drive a pro-metastatic transcriptional program distinct from that activated by canonical inflammatory signals.
proposals.method: We will use a panel of established human ovarian cancer cell lines (e.g., SKOV3, OVCAR3) cultured under physiologic (pH 7.4) and acidic (pH 6.5-6.8) conditions. Nuclear extracts will be analyzed for specific subunit DNA-binding activity using ELISA-based transcription factor assays (e.g., TransAM kits for NF-kappaB p65, p50, p52, RelB, c-Rel, and AP-1 subunits c-Fos, FosB, Fra-1, Fra-2, c-Jun, JunB, JunD). Dominant-negative constructs and siRNA-mediated knockdown of prioritized subunits will be used in functional assays for invasion (Matrigel) and endothelial tube formation (HUVEC co-culture). Correlative analysis of subunit expression with clinical outcomes will be performed using pre-existing RCC and ovarian cancer mRNA datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the precise molecular actors linking extracellular acidosis to aggressive tumor behavior in ovarian cancer. It will identify novel, context-specific therapeutic targets within the NF-kappaB and AP-1 pathways, moving beyond broad inhibition. The findings will provide a mechanistic framework for how the tumor microenvironment co-opts fundamental transcriptional regulators to promote metastasis.
proposals.novelty: While NF-kappaB/AP-1 activation by acidosis is known, this proposal seeks to identify the exact subunit composition, which is non-obvious and likely disease- and stimulus-specific. This subunit-level resolution, combined with Dr. Fidler's expertise in metastasis and angiogenesis, offers a novel strategy to disrupt pro-tumorigenic signaling with greater precision.

proposals.title: CRISPRi and Synthetic Reporter Screen Identifies the Acidosis-Responsive cis/trans Regulatory Axis for IL-8 mRNA Stabilization in Tumor Microenvironment
proposals.motivation: Tumor acidosis is a potent microenvironmental stressor that drives malignant progression, in part by upregulating pro-inflammatory and pro-angiogenic cytokines like IL-8. Pre-2026 work, including from our lab, shows acidosis stabilizes IL-8 mRNA, but the specific cis-elements and trans-acting RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) responsible remain undefined. Identifying this precise mechanism is critical for understanding how the microenvironment directly reprograms gene expression to promote metastasis and angiogenesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, modular cis-element within the IL-8 3'UTR is both necessary and sufficient for acidosis-induced mRNA stabilization, and that its function is mediated by one or more specific RBPs whose expression or activity is modulated by low pH.
proposals.method: We will construct a library of chimeric reporter mRNAs where a destabilizing element is fused to candidate IL-8 3'UTR fragments and measure their half-lives under normoxia vs. acidosis using a luciferase-based transcriptional pulse-chase system. In parallel, we will perform a focused CRISPR interference (CRISPRi) screen using a dCas9-KRAB library targeting the promoters of ~500 known/predicted human RBPs in endothelial and carcinoma cells. Hits will be validated by orthogonal knockdown and by electrophoretic mobility shift assays (EMSAs) under varying pH conditions to assess direct RBP-RNA interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first definitive mapping of the acidosis-responsive post-transcriptional regulon for IL-8, establishing direct causal links between specific RBPs, cis-elements, and microenvironmental signaling. It will identify novel, therapeutically targetable nodes that couple tumor metabolism to inflammatory gene expression, with implications for blocking metastasis and angiogenesis in cancers like renal cell carcinoma and colon cancer liver metastasis.
proposals.novelty: The combined use of synthetic, modular mRNA reporters to isolate cis-element function from confounding genomic context, coupled with a targeted CRISPRi screen for trans-factors, represents a novel orthogonal strategy to dissect post-transcriptional regulation by microenvironmental stress, moving beyond correlative observations to mechanistic validation.

proposals.title: Comparative RNA Interactome Capture Identifies Acidosis-Responsive RNA-Binding Proteins that Stabilize IL-8 mRNA in the Tumor Microenvironment
proposals.motivation: The acidic microenvironment of solid tumors drives aggressive progression and metastasis. A key mechanism is the acidosis-induced stabilization of pro-angiogenic and pro-inflammatory cytokine mRNAs, such as IL-8, which promotes tumor growth and angiogenesis. However, the specific RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) that mediate this pH-sensitive mRNA stabilization remain largely unknown, representing a critical gap in understanding tumor-immune communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that comparative RNA interactome capture (RIC) under physiological versus acidic conditions will identify a specific set of acidosis-induced RBPs that bind to the IL-8 3'UTR, thereby elucidating the core post-transcriptional mechanism driving IL-8 overexpression in the acidic tumor niche.
proposals.method: We will adapt the comparative RIC protocol, analogous to plant hypoxia screens, to human endothelial or prostate cancer cells cultured at pH 7.4 versus pH 6.5. Following UV crosslinking, poly(A)+ mRNA-protein complexes will be captured with oligo(dT) beads, and bound proteins identified by quantitative mass spectrometry (e.g., TMT labeling). Bioinformatic analysis using the RBP2GO pipeline and the POSTAR2 database will prioritize candidate RBPs. Validation will involve RIP-qPCR for IL-8 mRNA binding and luciferase reporter assays with IL-8 3'UTR truncations to map functional cis-elements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unbiased map of the acidosis-responsive mRNA interactome in human cells, directly identifying novel regulators of cytokine expression in the tumor microenvironment. It will establish a mechanistic link between extracellular acidosis and IL-8-driven angiogenesis, offering potential new targets (the identified RBPs or their binding sites) for therapeutic intervention in metastatic cancers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of a plant stress biology tool (comparative RIC) to solve a long-standing problem in cancer biology, enabling an unbiased, systems-level discovery of condition-specific mRNA stabilizers, a task not achievable through candidate-gene approaches.

proposals.title: Causal Manipulation of Intracellular pH Reveals Its Role as a Pro-Metastatic Signaling Node in Tumor Cell IL-8 Expression
proposals.motivation: The cytokine IL-8 is a potent promoter of tumor angiogenesis and metastasis. While correlative studies link acidic tumor microenvironments and intracellular pH (pHi) dynamics to increased IL-8 expression, a direct causal relationship remains unproven. Determining if pHi is a driver or a bystander in IL-8 induction is critical for understanding the mechanistic basis of inflammation-driven cancer progression and for evaluating pH modulation as a therapeutic strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, compartmentalized alkalinization of the cytosol is a necessary and sufficient early signal for the transcriptional upregulation of IL-8 in cancer cells exposed to pro-inflammatory or hypoxic stimuli.
proposals.method: We will engineer human cancer cell lines (e.g., colon carcinoma) with an integrated biosensor-actuator system: a ratiometric pH biosensor (pHluorin2) for real-time imaging and an orthogonal, optogenetic pH actuator (Archon2, a light-gated proton pump). Using time-lapse microscopy, we will clamp cytosolic pH at defined setpoints via 590 nm light before and during stimulation with TNF-alpha or hypoxia mimetics. IL-8 mRNA will be quantified by qRT-PCR, and secretion measured by ELISA. Parallel experiments will use a chemically inducible system (FKBP-FRB linked to a plasma membrane proton transporter) for validation in vivo in a liver metastasis model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive causal link between intracellular pH dynamics and a key pro-metastatic cytokine. It will identify whether pH acts as a master regulator or a context-dependent modulator of IL-8, directly informing the therapeutic potential of targeting tumor acid-base balance. The integrated biosensor-actuator platform provides a novel methodology for dissecting ionic signaling in cancer biology.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond correlative pH measurements, this study employs precise, real-time pH manipulation to test causality, a significant advance over prior pharmacologic approaches that lack spatiotemporal control. The integration of optogenetic actuators with metastasis biology represents a novel fusion of biophysical and oncological techniques.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Coupling of Tumor Microenvironment Acidosis to Pro-Metastatic Transcription Factor Activation in Human Cancer Cells
proposals.motivation: The acidic tumor microenvironment, a hallmark of solid cancers, is a potent driver of invasion and metastasis, partly through the upregulation of pro-angiogenic and pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-8. While acidosis is known to activate AP-1 and NF-κB, the precise spatiotemporal dynamics linking specific intracellular pH changes to the activation of a broader network of metastasis-associated transcription factors (TFs) remain poorly defined. This gap limits our ability to therapeutically target pH-sensitive transcriptional programs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extracellular acidosis triggers rapid, compartment-specific intracellular pH shifts that sequentially activate a defined network of pro-metastatic transcription factors, including HIF-1α, ATF, and NFAT, with distinct temporal kinetics that drive IL-8 expression and other pro-metastatic phenotypes.
proposals.method: We will use lentiviral transduction to stably express a panel of genetically encoded, ratiometric pH biosensors (e.g., pHluorin, pHRed) targeted to the cytoplasm and nucleus in human endothelial and colon carcinoma cell lines. These will be co-expressed with fluorescent protein-based transcription factor activity reporters (e.g., HIF-1α, NFAT, AP-1) using distinct fluorophores. Live-cell, time-lapse confocal microscopy will be performed on cells subjected to controlled acidic stress (pH 6.5-6.8). Image analysis will utilize established algorithms (e.g., CellProfiler for segmentation, custom MATLAB/FIJI scripts for ratiometric quantification) to correlate spatial pH dynamics with TF nuclear translocation/activity in single cells over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first direct, causal timeline linking tumor microenvironment acidosis to the activation of specific pro-metastatic transcription factors. It will identify which TFs are primary pH sensors versus secondary responders, providing a mechanistic map of acidosis-induced signaling. This could reveal novel, temporally specific therapeutic windows for disrupting pH-driven metastasis.
proposals.novelty: The simultaneous, multiplexed live-cell imaging of compartmentalized pH dynamics and multiple TF activities moves beyond correlative, endpoint assays to establish direct kinetic relationships, enabling the discovery of pH-sensitive transcriptional networks rather than individual factors.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific 3D Chromatin Remodeling of the IL-8 Locus by Tumor Microenvironment Acidosis in Human Endothelial Subtypes
proposals.motivation: The acidic tumor microenvironment is a potent driver of inflammation and angiogenesis, key processes in metastasis. IL-8 is a critical pro-angiogenic and pro-metastatic chemokine strongly induced by acidosis in endothelial cells, but its regulation is heterogeneous across vascular beds, influencing organ-specific metastasis. Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Fidler's work on angiogenesis and metastasis, have not defined how the 3D genome architecture of endothelial cells, a fundamental regulator of gene expression, is altered by acidosis in a cell-type-specific manner.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acidosis induces cell-type-specific 3D chromatin conformational changes at the IL-8 locus in endothelial cells, and that divergent architectural remodeling underlies the differential inflammatory vulnerability of macrovascular, microvascular, and arterial endothelia.
proposals.method: We will apply in situ Hi-C and promoter-specific 3C (4C-seq) to HUVEC (macrovascular), human dermal microvascular endothelial cells (HDMEC), and human coronary artery endothelial cells (HCAEC) under physiologic (pH 7.4) and acidic (pH 6.5-6.8) conditions. Hi-C data will be processed using the HiC-Pro pipeline and analyzed with Fit-Hi-C to identify significant chromatin loops and topologically associating domains (TADs). Differential interaction analysis will be performed using diffHic. 4C-seq profiles will validate specific IL-8 promoter-enhancer interactions. Architectural changes will be integrated with RNA-seq and histone modification (H3K27ac) ChIP-seq data from the same conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comparative map of acidosis-induced 3D genome reorganization in endothelial subtypes, directly linking extracellular pH to architectural control of a major metastatic gene. It will establish whether conserved or distinct structural mechanisms govern inflammatory responses in different vascular beds, providing a novel architectural dimension to the concept of organ-specific endothelial regulation in metastasis.
proposals.novelty: While acidosis-induced IL-8 expression is known, the 3D chromatin basis of this regulation is unexplored. The comparative approach across functionally distinct, primary human endothelial cell types—integrating microenvironmental stress with cell-type-specific genome architecture—is novel and moves beyond single-cell-type studies.

proposals.title: Acidosis-Induced Chromatin Remodeling of the IL-8 Promoter in Tumor-Associated Endothelial Cells: A 3C-Based Analysis of Long-Range Enhancer-Promoter Interactions
proposals.motivation: The acidic tumor microenvironment is a potent driver of angiogenesis and metastasis, partly through the upregulation of pro-angiogenic factors like IL-8 in endothelial cells. While acidosis is known to increase IL-8 mRNA expression via transcription factors like NF-κB, the precise three-dimensional chromatin architecture governing this response remains unexplored. Understanding how acidosis alters promoter conformation and long-range regulatory interactions is critical for deciphering the epigenetic mechanisms of therapy resistance in metastatic cancers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extracellular acidosis induces specific, long-range chromatin interactions between the IL-8 promoter and distal enhancer elements in human tumor-associated endothelial cells, and that this conformational change is necessary for the sustained, high-level IL-8 expression that drives pathological angiogenesis.
proposals.method: We will apply Chromatin Conformation Capture (3C) and its high-throughput variant, Hi-C, to human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and patient-derived tumor endothelial cells cultured under physiological (pH 7.4) or acidic (pH 6.5-6.8) conditions. The IL-8 promoter will serve as the viewpoint for 3C-qPCR validation of specific interactions. Hi-C data will be processed using the HiC-Pro pipeline and analyzed with Fit-Hi-C to identify significant acidosis-induced chromatin loops. Interactions will be correlated with H3K27ac ChIP-seq and ATAC-seq data to define active enhancers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence linking the acidic tumor microenvironment to specific, long-range chromatin structural changes in endothelial cells. It will identify novel, acidosis-responsive enhancer elements regulating IL-8, offering new potential targets for epigenetic or anti-angiogenic therapy. The findings will extend the 'seed and soil' metastasis paradigm into the realm of 3D genome architecture.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Fidler's prior work established the role of soluble factors like IFN-α in suppressing angiogenesis, this proposal investigates a physical microenvironmental cue (acidosis) as a direct modulator of chromatin structure, a novel mechanistic angle. Applying 3C-based methods to study endothelial cell adaptation to acidosis is an unexplored synthesis of biophysics and 3D genomics in cancer biology.

proposals.title: Mapping the Acidosis-Induced IL-8 mRNA Interactome in Tumor Endothelial Cells Using Targeted Proximity Labeling
proposals.motivation: Tumor microenvironment acidosis is a potent driver of angiogenesis and metastasis, in part by stabilizing pro-angiogenic mRNAs like IL-8 in endothelial cells. However, the complete network of RNA-binding proteins and regulatory factors that control IL-8 mRNA stability under acidic stress remains poorly characterized, limiting our ability to therapeutically target this pathway. Traditional biochemical pulldowns fail to capture the weak, transient, and pH-dependent interactions that are critical for this regulation within the native cellular context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fusing the engineered biotin ligase TurboID to an RNA-binding domain specific for IL-8 mRNA will enable the capture of a distinct, pH-dependent protein interactome that governs mRNA stability during tumor acidosis.
proposals.method: We will stably express a fusion protein consisting of TurboID and the MS2 bacteriophage coat protein (MCP) in human tumor endothelial cells. These cells will harbor an IL-8 mRNA transcript engineered with MS2 stem-loops in its 3' UTR. Following culture at physiological (pH 7.4) or acidic (pH 6.5) conditions, proximity biotinylation will be triggered. Biotinylated proteins will be isolated via stringent streptavidin pulldown and identified by quantitative tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). Differential interactors will be validated by co-immunoprecipitation and their functional role in IL-8 mRNA stability assessed using actinomycin D chase assays upon siRNA knockdown.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first spatially resolved, in vivo crosslinking map of the IL-8 mRNA regulatory complex under acidosis. It will identify novel pH-sensing RNA-binding proteins and co-factors, providing mechanistic insight into post-transcriptional regulation in the tumor microenvironment. These findings could reveal new targets for disrupting pathological angiogenesis in cancers such as renal cell carcinoma and colon cancer metastasis.
proposals.novelty: This adapts proximity-labeling tools from chromatin biology (e.g., ChIC) to the specific challenge of mapping the dynamic mRNA-protein interactome in response to a physiological tumor stressor, moving beyond static in vitro RNA affinity purifications.

proposals.title: Identification of pH-Dependent IL-8 mRNA Binding Proteins in Tumor-Associated Endothelial Cells: A Mechanism for the Acidic Tumor Microenvironment-Driven Angiogenic Switch
proposals.motivation: The acidic tumor microenvironment, a hallmark of solid tumors, promotes angiogenesis and metastasis, in part by upregulating pro-angiogenic cytokines like IL-8. Pre-2026 work by Dr. Fidler and others established that systemic interferon-alpha can suppress angiogenesis, but the post-transcriptional mechanisms by which acidosis stabilizes key mRNAs like IL-8 in endothelial cells remain poorly defined. Identifying the RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) that control IL-8 mRNA fate under acidic stress is critical for understanding therapy resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the acidic tumor microenvironment induces the binding of specific RNA-binding proteins to the IL-8 mRNA transcript, enhancing its stability and translation in human tumor-associated endothelial cells, thereby driving an angiogenic switch.
proposals.method: We will employ in vitro RNA pulldown assays using in vitro transcribed, biotinylated full-length human IL-8 mRNA. Lysates from human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) cultured at normal physiological pH (7.4) versus acidic pH (6.5) will be incubated with the bait RNA. Captured protein complexes will be isolated using streptavidin beads, eluted, and identified via liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). Differential binding candidates will be validated by RNA immunoprecipitation (RIP) and siRNA-mediated knockdown to assess functional impact on IL-8 mRNA half-life (measured by qRT-PCR after actinomycin D treatment) and endothelial tube formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel post-transcriptional regulatory axis linking tumor acidosis to angiogenesis. It will provide a specific catalog of pH-sensitive IL-8 mRNA interactors in endothelial cells, offering new potential targets for anti-angiogenic therapy. The findings will extend the mechanistic understanding of microenvironmental control of gene expression beyond transcription, a core theme in metastasis research.
proposals.novelty: While IL-8's role in angiogenesis is known, the specific RBPs that mediate its pH-dependent regulation are unidentified. Applying a direct mRNA pulldown approach to compare protein binding under acidic versus normal conditions in endothelial cells integrates Dr. Fidler's expertise in the tumor microenvironment and angiogenesis with contemporary RNA-protein interaction methods.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Screening Identifies Novel Pharmacologic Inhibitors of Tumor Microenvironment Acidosis-Induced IL-8 Expression in Human Cancer Cells
proposals.motivation: The acidic tumor microenvironment, a hallmark of solid tumors, drives cancer progression and metastasis by inducing pro-angiogenic and pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-8. Pre-2026 work, including from our lab, shows that acidosis potently upregulates IL-8 expression, promoting endothelial cell recruitment and angiogenesis. However, no targeted pharmacological strategies exist to specifically disrupt this critical pH-sensing pathway in cancer cells.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific small-molecule compounds can be identified that selectively block the transcriptional upregulation of IL-8 in response to extracellular acidosis, without affecting basal IL-8 expression under normal pH, thereby uncoupling the tumor's adaptive response to its acidic niche.
proposals.method: We will perform a high-throughput screen using a human prostate carcinoma cell line (PC-3) stably transfected with an IL-8 promoter-driven luciferase reporter construct. Cells will be exposed to acidic (pH 6.8) versus normal (pH 7.4) medium in 384-well plates pre-dosed with ~10,000 compounds from the NIH Clinical Collection and Mechanistic Set libraries. Luminescence will be quantified after 24 hours. Hits will be validated by ELISA for secreted IL-8 protein and qRT-PCR for IL-8 mRNA in multiple carcinoma cell lines. Secondary assays will assess specificity by measuring acidosis-induced VEGF expression and cytotoxicity via MTS assay.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield the first panel of pharmacologic agents specifically targeting the acidosis-IL-8 axis, a key microenvironmental driver of angiogenesis. It will provide novel chemical tools to dissect the pH-sensing signaling pathway and establish proof-of-concept for a new therapeutic strategy aimed at 'normalizing' the tumor microenvironment to suppress metastasis.
proposals.novelty: While previous screens have sought anti-angiogenic or anti-IL-8 agents, none have specifically used the physiological trigger of extracellular acidosis as the screening condition to find compounds that disrupt the microenvironmental induction mechanism itself.

proposals.title: Systemic Evaluation of Alpha-N-Acetylgalactosaminidase (Nagalase) as a Tumor-Specific Biomarker: Distinguishing Malignant from Inflammatory Sources in Human Cancers
proposals.motivation: Alpha-N-acetylgalactosaminidase (nagalase) serum activity is a proposed biomarker for cancer progression, linked to degradation of vitamin D-binding protein and immunosuppression. However, its specificity for malignancy versus general inflammation is contested in pre-2026 literature, with some studies suggesting elevation in chronic inflammatory conditions. Clarifying the cellular origin of elevated nagalase is critical for its validation as a reliable, tumor-derived prognostic and therapy-response indicator in oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: Elevated serum nagalase activity in cancer patients is derived specifically from tumor cells and tumor-associated myeloid cells, and not from systemic inflammation or non-malignant tissues. Inflammatory conditions alone will not produce significant, sustained nagalase elevation.
proposals.method: We will analyze matched human serum, tumor tissue, and peripheral blood leukocytes (PBLs) from cohorts of prostate, colon, and renal cell carcinoma patients (archived samples from prior studies). Nagalase enzymatic activity will be measured via fluorogenic substrate. We will correlate serum levels with: 1) tumor burden/metastasis (imaging), 2) intratumoral nagalase mRNA expression (RNA-seq/nanostring), and 3) systemic inflammatory markers (e.g., CRP, IL-6). A key parallel study will measure nagalase in patients with severe non-malignant inflammation (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis, sepsis). Single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics) on dissociated tumors and PBLs will identify the specific cell populations (tumor, endothelial, macrophage) expressing NAGA.
proposals.contribution: This study will definitively test the tumor-specificity of nagalase, providing essential validation for its use as a cancer biomarker. It will delineate the contribution of tumor versus host cells to circulating nagalase, linking it to Dr. Fidler's expertise in tumor-stroma interactions. The results could establish nagalase as a more specific tool than general inflammatory markers for monitoring therapeutic efficacy, such as interferon-alpha or docetaxel regimens.
proposals.novelty: While nagalase is studied in cancer, a systematic, multi-cancer analysis integrating serum activity, single-cell transcriptional profiling from tumors, and direct comparison to pure inflammatory states is lacking. This approach directly tests the core assumption that nagalase is a tumor-derived factor, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic origin.

proposals.title: Prospective Validation of Serum Alpha-N-Acetylgalactosaminidase (Nagalase) Activity as a Dynamic Biomarker for Metastatic Burden and Response to Interferon-alpha Therapy in Renal Cell Carcinoma
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies suggest alpha-N-acetylgalactosaminidase (nagalase), an enzyme secreted by many cancers, degrades vitamin D-binding protein, impairing macrophage activation and potentially facilitating immune evasion. Elevated serum nagalase activity correlates with tumor burden in several cancers, but its utility as a dynamic, predictive biomarker for treatment response, particularly to immunomodulatory agents like interferon-alpha (IFN-α), remains unvalidated in prospective trials. Given Dr. Fidler's expertise in metastasis, angiogenesis, and IFN-α therapy, validating a non-invasive biomarker for monitoring disease could significantly impact clinical management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that serum nagalase activity will correlate with metastatic tumor burden in renal cell carcinoma (RCC) patients and that a rapid decline in nagalase following systemic IFN-α therapy will predict improved progression-free and overall survival.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, longitudinal study in RCC patients with measurable liver metastases scheduled for IFN-α therapy (as per prior Fidler protocols). Serum nagalase activity will be quantified using a fluorometric enzyme assay at baseline, weekly for one month, and monthly thereafter. Tumor burden will be assessed via volumetric MRI. Nagalase dynamics will be correlated with radiographic response (RECIST 1.1), progression-free survival (PFS), and overall survival (OS). Statistical analysis will employ Cox proportional hazards models, treating nagalase change as a time-dependent covariate, and ROC analysis to determine predictive cut-offs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prospective, mechanistic validation of nagalase as a dynamic, pharmacodynamic biomarker for IFN-α response in metastatic RCC. It will establish a potential clinical tool for early assessment of therapeutic efficacy and disease monitoring, bridging the gap between tumor biology (immune suppression, angiogenesis) and patient outcome. The findings could inform future trials of nagalase-targeted therapies.
proposals.novelty: While nagalase has been explored in cross-sectional studies, its prospective validation as a dynamic, treatment-response biomarker, specifically in the context of a defined anti-angiogenic/immunomodulatory therapy (IFN-α) in a metastasis model, is novel and directly extends Dr. Fidler's published work on IFN-α and metastatic progression.

proposals.title: Comparative Efficacy of IL-8 Receptor Antagonists versus Neutralizing Antibodies in Suppressing the Metastatic Phenotype of Human Renal Cell Carcinoma
proposals.motivation: Interleukin-8 (IL-8) is a potent pro-angiogenic and pro-metastatic chemokine implicated in the progression of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) and other solid tumors. Pre-2026 clinical strategies to inhibit the IL-8 axis are bifurcating, with both small-molecule CXCR1/2 antagonists and monoclonal neutralizing antibodies in development. A direct, mechanistic comparison of these therapeutic modalities is lacking but critical for guiding optimal clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that small-molecule CXCR1/2 receptor antagonists will be more effective than antibody-based IL-8 ligand neutralization in suppressing tumor growth, angiogenesis, and metastasis due to their ability to block signaling from all CXCR1/2 ligands and potentially penetrate the tumor microenvironment more efficiently.
proposals.method: We will utilize established human RCC cell lines (e.g., 786-O, Caki-1) with high endogenous IL-8 expression, as confirmed by mRNA quantification and ELISA. In vitro, we will compare the effects of a specific CXCR1/2 antagonist (e.g., Reparixin) and a neutralizing anti-IL-8 antibody on endothelial cell tube formation (HUVEC co-culture assays) and tumor cell invasion (Boyden chamber). In vivo, using our well-characterized orthotopic kidney tumor and experimental lung metastasis models in nude mice, we will administer systemic therapy with each agent and quantify primary tumor growth, microvessel density (CD31 immunohistochemistry), and lung metastatic burden. mRNA expression profiles of angiogenesis (VEGF, bFGF) and metastasis (MMP-9) regulators will be analyzed from harvested tumors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, head-to-head preclinical evidence defining the superior therapeutic modality for targeting the IL-8 pathway in RCC. It will elucidate whether blocking the receptor or the ligand is more effective in disrupting key hallmarks of cancer, directly informing the design of future combination therapies and clinical trials.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Fidler's prior work established the correlation of angiogenic/metastatic genes with RCC stage and the utility of systemic interferon-alpha, this proposal innovatively applies his metastasis model expertise to a direct, mechanistic comparison of two distinct pharmacological strategies targeting the same cytokine pathway, a question not addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Dynamic Bioluminescence Imaging of Melanoma Metastasis to Quantify the Efficacy of Interferon-Alpha and Anti-Angiogenic Therapy
proposals.motivation: Metastasis is the primary cause of cancer mortality, yet quantifying its real-time progression and response to therapy remains a major challenge. Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Fidler's own studies, demonstrates the critical roles of angiogenesis and cytokine signaling (e.g., IFN-α) in metastatic growth, but these findings rely on endpoint histology, missing the dynamic biology of spread.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal, quantitative bioluminescence imaging of luciferase-tagged melanoma cells will reveal distinct patterns of metastatic spread and organ tropism, and that these patterns will be differentially and dynamically suppressed by systemic interferon-alpha and anti-angiogenic therapy.
proposals.method: Human melanoma cell lines (e.g., A375) will be stably transduced with a lentiviral vector expressing firefly luciferase. Cells will be injected into immunodeficient mice via the tail vein (experimental metastasis) or orthotopically (spontaneous metastasis). Metastatic burden will be serially quantified using the IVIS Spectrum in vivo imaging system. Mice will be treated with systemic recombinant human IFN-α or a VEGF pathway inhibitor (e.g., bevacizumab). Bioluminescence flux (photons/sec) from defined regions of interest (lungs, liver, bone) will be correlated with endpoint histology and mRNA expression of metastatic (e.g., MMPs) and angiogenic (VEGF, PDGF) regulators from isolated tumor cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a dynamic, quantitative model for assessing metastatic progression and therapy response in real time, moving beyond static snapshots. It will directly link Dr. Fidler's expertise in metastasis, angiogenesis, and cytokine therapy to a powerful functional imaging readout, providing kinetic data on how these therapies alter the fate of disseminated cells.
proposals.novelty: While bioluminescence imaging is established, its application here is novel for specifically testing IFN-α and anti-angiogenics in a kinetic model of human melanoma metastasis, allowing for the dissection of therapy effects on the rate of seeding versus growth at secondary sites.

proposals.title: Serum IL-8 as a Prognostic Biomarker for Metastatic Progression and Survival in Renal Cell Carcinoma: A Validation Study
proposals.motivation: The clinical management of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is hindered by a lack of reliable, non-invasive biomarkers to predict metastatic potential and patient outcome. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Fidler's own work, has established that interleukin-8 (IL-8) is a key cytokine promoting tumor angiogenesis, metastasis, and therapy resistance in multiple cancers. Validating a serum-based assay for IL-8 could provide a critical tool for risk stratification and therapeutic monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that elevated baseline serum levels of IL-8, measured by ELISA, correlate with advanced pathological stage, increased risk of metastasis, and shorter overall survival in patients with RCC.
proposals.method: We will apply a quantitative sandwich ELISA (Human IL-8/CXCL8 DuoSet ELISA, R&D Systems) to banked serum samples from a well-annotated cohort of RCC patients with known clinical outcomes. The assay will be performed in duplicate, with optical density measured using a microplate reader. Serum IL-8 concentrations will be correlated with clinicopathological variables (stage, grade, metastasis status) using non-parametric tests (Mann-Whitney U, Kruskal-Wallis). Survival analysis (Kaplan-Meier curves, log-rank test, and Cox proportional hazards regression) will determine the prognostic value of IL-8 levels for progression-free and overall survival.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct clinical validation for IL-8 as a circulating prognostic biomarker in RCC, linking a key mediator of the metastatic phenotype to patient outcomes. It will establish a practical, serum-based assay that could be integrated into clinical trials for patient stratification or as a pharmacodynamic marker for therapies targeting angiogenesis or the IL-8 axis.
proposals.novelty: While IL-8's role in cancer biology is known, its validation as a standalone, pre-treatment serum prognostic marker in RCC, specifically linking it to metastatic progression and survival in a clinical cohort, remains underexplored and represents a direct translational application of mechanistic findings.

proposals.title: Acidic Tumor Microenvironment Reprograms Cytokine Signaling by Altering the Temporal Dynamics of mRNA-Protein Interactomes
proposals.motivation: The sequence and timing of cytokine exposure, a 'temporal cytokine code,' critically determines cellular responses in processes like metastasis and angiogenesis. The acidic extracellular pH of the tumor microenvironment is a known driver of cancer progression and therapy resistance, but its impact on post-transcriptional regulation of cytokine-induced genes remains poorly understood. This project directly addresses how acidosis modifies the fundamental molecular interpreters of cytokine timing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional outcome of sequential cytokine signaling is dictated by pH-dependent remodeling of specific mRNA-protein interactomes on key cytokine-induced transcripts, and that these dynamic protein-binding events are required effectors of the temporal code.
proposals.method: We will use human endothelial cells and established cancer cell lines. Cells will be exposed to defined sequences of relevant cytokines (e.g., TNF-α followed by IFN-α) under normal (pH 7.4) or acidic (pH 6.8) conditions. At precise timepoints, we will perform biotinylated mRNA pulldowns for target transcripts (e.g., IL-8, VEGF-A) using the MS2-MBP system. Captured protein complexes will be identified by quantitative mass spectrometry (TMT labeling). Bioinformatic analysis will use established algorithms (e.g., DAVID for pathway enrichment, STRING for network analysis) to compare interactome composition and dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will map, for the first time, how extracellular pH sculpts the post-transcriptional regulatory landscape in response to cytokine sequences. It will identify novel pH-sensitive RNA-binding proteins as potential therapeutic targets to disrupt pathological signaling in the tumor microenvironment. The findings will provide a mechanistic link between acidosis and altered cytokine responses in cancer progression.
proposals.novelty: While cytokine sequences and tumor acidosis are both studied separately, their convergence on mRNA-protein interactome dynamics is unexplored. This moves beyond measuring cytokine presence or mRNA expression levels to directly interrogate the effector complexes that execute the temporal code.

proposals.title: Tumor-Specific Rewiring of the Angiogenic Cascade: Cytokine Hierarchies Dictate Coupling Between Endothelial Migration and Tube Formation
proposals.motivation: Anti-angiogenic therapies targeting single pathways (e.g., VEGF) have shown limited efficacy and resistance, suggesting a more complex, tumor-specific regulation of blood vessel formation. The field lacks a systematic understanding of how the core steps of angiogenesis are functionally linked in different cancers. This gap hinders the development of more effective, context-specific anti-angiogenic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The interdependence of sequential angiogenic steps is not a fixed biological program but is dynamically rewired by the unique cytokine hierarchy of a tumor's microenvironment, leading to cancer-specific patterns of coupling between endothelial cell migration and tube formation.
proposals.method: We will isolate primary tumor-derived endothelial cells (TECs) from human renal cell carcinoma, colon cancer liver metastases, and prostate cancer xenografts, comparing them to HUVECs and normal tissue endothelial cells. Using a high-content imaging platform, we will quantify migration (scratch assay) and tube formation (Matrigel) under baseline conditions and after systematic perturbation with specific inhibitors (e.g., PDGFR, VEGFR, or IFN-α pathway modulators). Coupling strength will be calculated as the proportional change in one step per unit inhibition of another. We will correlate these coupling maps with comprehensive cytokine response profiles generated via multiplex ELISA and mRNA expression analysis of angiogenic regulators from TEC lysates.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first functional map of angiogenic step coupling across different cancers, directly linking it to tumor-specific signaling hierarchies. It will identify novel, context-dependent therapeutic targets by revealing which step interdependencies are critical vulnerabilities in specific tumor types, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all approach to angiogenesis inhibition.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Fidler and others has shown tumor-specific endothelial phenotypes and the importance of cytokines like IFN-α, this proposal uniquely tests the malleable *logic* of the angiogenic program itself, positing that the tumor microenvironment can rewire core endothelial cell biology.

proposals.title: Genetic Determinants of Angiogenic Program Modularity in Ovarian Cancer: BRCA, p53, and KRAS Status Dictate the Coupling of Endothelial Migration and Tube Formation
proposals.motivation: Anti-angiogenic therapy, targeting factors like VEGF, has shown variable and often limited efficacy in ovarian cancer, suggesting tumor-specific resistance. Pre-2026 work indicates angiogenesis is a multi-step process, but it is unknown if these steps are universally interdependent or if their coupling is dictated by the tumor's genetic landscape. Understanding this context-dependency is crucial for developing more effective, personalized anti-angiogenic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The genetic background of ovarian cancer cells, specifically mutations in BRCA, p53, and KRAS, determines the functional interdependence of distinct angiogenic steps, such that inhibition of one step (e.g., endothelial migration) will have a variable, genotype-specific impact on subsequent steps (e.g., tube formation).
proposals.method: We will use a panel of well-characterized human ovarian cancer cell lines (e.g., OVCAR, SKOV, A2780) with defined mutations in BRCA, p53, and KRAS. Conditioned media from these lines will be used in standardized in vitro angiogenesis assays with human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs). We will pharmacologically inhibit specific steps (e.g., using the Src inhibitor PP2 to disrupt migration) and quantitatively measure downstream outcomes (tube formation on Matrigel) via automated image analysis (ImageJ Angiogenesis Analyzer). mRNA expression of key angiogenic regulators (VEGF, IL-8, PDGF) from the cancer cell lines will be correlated with functional assay results.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a functional map linking specific ovarian cancer driver mutations to the modularity of angiogenic programs. It will identify genetic contexts where angiogenesis is a monolithic, easily disrupted process versus a resilient, modular one. This provides a mechanistic rationale for patient stratification and for targeting non-canonical angiogenic pathways in defined genetic subsets.
proposals.novelty: While genetic drivers are known to influence angiogenic factor expression, this study directly tests the novel concept that they re-wire the functional logic of the angiogenic program itself, determining whether its steps are coupled or independent. It moves beyond correlative expression studies to a causal, functional interrogation of angiogenic network topology.

proposals.title: Real-Time Correlation of Acidosis-Induced NF-κB/HIF-1α Signaling and IL-8 Promoter Conformation in Live Tumor Cells
proposals.motivation: The acidic tumor microenvironment is a key driver of metastasis, promoting pro-inflammatory and pro-angiogenic gene expression like IL-8. While acidosis activates pathways such as NF-κB and HIF-1α, the causal, temporal relationship between signaling activation and the dynamic 3D chromatin remodeling of target promoters remains unknown. Establishing this causality is critical to understanding how the physical microenvironment directly reprograms the cancer cell's transcriptional landscape to drive aggressive phenotypes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extracellular acidosis triggers rapid, specific conformational changes in the IL-8 promoter-enhancer interactome, and that these structural remodeling events are direct, temporally ordered consequences of NF-κB and HIF-1α signaling pathway activation within the nucleus.
proposals.method: We will engineer human prostate or colon carcinoma cell lines to stably express FRET-based biosensors for nuclear NF-κB and HIF-1α activity. In parallel, we will use a live-cell compatible CRISPR/dCas9 imaging system with orthogonal fluorescent tags (e.g., MS2/PP7 stem-loops) to label the IL-8 promoter and a key distal enhancer. Cells will be imaged in a controlled microenvironment chamber under physiological (pH 7.4) and acidic (pH 6.5-6.8) conditions using high-resolution, multi-channel 4D confocal microscopy. Custom computational pipelines (using existing tools like TrackMate and Fiji) will quantify the co-localization and distance dynamics between labeled loci alongside FRET ratio changes over time in single cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, single-cell temporal map linking an extracellular tumor microenvironmental cue to intracellular signaling kinetics and subsequent nanoscale structural changes in chromatin. It will establish a causal hierarchy between pathway activation and gene conformation, offering a new mechanistic framework for how acidosis promotes metastasis-associated gene expression.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous, live-cell observation of signaling biochemistry and specific chromatin architecture dynamics in response to a physiologically relevant tumor stressor, moving beyond static, population-averaged 3C/Hi-C data to a 4D (3D + time) causal model.

proposals.title: Modeling the Acidic Tumor Microenvironment's Control of Metastasis: A pH-Dependent RNA-Protein Interactome and Network Analysis of IL-8 mRNA Stability
proposals.motivation: The acidic tumor microenvironment, a hallmark of solid tumors, drives cancer progression and metastasis, in part by upregulating pro-angiogenic and pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-8. Pre-2026 knowledge indicates that post-transcriptional regulation, particularly mRNA stability, is a critical but poorly understood control point for cytokine expression in response to extracellular pH. A systems-level understanding of how acidity remodels the mRNA regulatory network is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extracellular acidosis induces a specific re-wiring of the IL-8 mRNA-protein interactome, creating a post-transcriptional network whose key regulatory nodes can be computationally modeled to predict cytokine output and identify novel therapeutic targets for metastatic disease.
proposals.method: We will perform biotinylated IL-8 mRNA pulldowns with lysates from human endothelial or carcinoma cells cultured under normal (pH 7.4) versus acidic (pH 6.5-6.8) conditions, followed by mass spectrometry to identify pH-dependent RNA-binding proteins (RBPs). This experimental interactome data will parameterize a computational network model built using a Boolean or ordinary differential equation (ODE) framework. The model will simulate network dynamics, predicting IL-8 mRNA half-life changes upon node perturbations (e.g., RBP knockdown), which will be validated via actinomycin-D chase assays and qPCR.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind, pH-parameterized network model of cytokine post-transcriptional regulation, directly linking tumor microenvironmental stress to a predictive computational framework. It will identify novel, therapeutically targetable RBPs that control metastasis-associated cytokine expression, extending Dr. Fidler's legacy in angiogenesis and metastasis therapy.
proposals.novelty: While mRNA stability networks and tumor acidity have been studied separately, this project's novelty lies in the direct integration of context-specific (pH-modulated) experimental interactomics into a predictive computational model, creating a closed-loop discovery platform for microenvironment-driven post-transcriptional regulation.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Cytokine Inhibition in the Metastatic Cascade: Targeting VEGF, PDGF, and FGF for Stage-Specific Suppression of Angiogenesis in Liver Metastasis Models
proposals.motivation: Broad-spectrum anti-angiogenic therapies often fail due to tumor adaptation and compensatory cytokine signaling. Dr. Fidler's prior work with IFN-α demonstrated the potential of cytokine-targeted therapy, but a more refined approach is needed. This proposal addresses the critical need to understand and therapeutically exploit the sequential, non-redundant roles of specific cytokines (e.g., VEGF, PDGF, FGF) during distinct steps of the angiogenic switch in established metastases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the progression of angiogenesis in established metastases depends on a temporally ordered cascade of cytokine activities, and that targeted inhibition of the dominant cytokine at each sequential step (endothelial activation, proliferation, stabilization) will yield superior and more durable anti-metastatic effects compared to pan-inhibition or single-agent therapy.
proposals.method: We will use human colon carcinoma cell lines (e.g., KM12SM) to establish liver metastases in nude mice. At defined time points post-colonization, we will profile cytokine and receptor expression (VEGFR, PDGFR, FGFR) in metastatic lesions and associated endothelial cells using mRNA sequencing and phospho-protein arrays. Based on this spatiotemporal map, we will treat cohorts with stage-specific, clinically relevant monoclonal antibodies (e.g., bevacizumab for VEGF, olaratumab for PDGF-α) or small-molecule kinase inhibitors, administered systemically at the timepoints indicated by the profiling data. Therapeutic efficacy will be assessed by metastasis number/size, microvessel density (CD31), and perfusion imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a functional map of cytokine dependency during metastatic angiogenesis, moving beyond static biomarker correlations. It will provide pre-clinical proof-of-concept for a dynamic, sequential therapeutic regimen. The findings could directly inform the design of adaptive clinical trials for metastatic disease, shifting the paradigm from static to temporally-informed anti-angiogenic therapy.
proposals.novelty: While cytokine roles in angiogenesis are known, the proposal to therapeutically target them in a temporally precise sequence based on the evolving biology of the metastatic lesion is novel. It integrates Dr. Fidler's expertise in metastasis biology and cytokine therapy with a dynamic, stepwise pharmacological strategy not yet explored in his published work.

proposals.title: Epigenetic Silencing of Inducible Nitric Oxide Synthase (iNOS) in Metastatic Melanoma: A Mechanism for Evading Nitric Oxide-Mediated Growth Suppression
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research indicates that nitric oxide (NO), produced by iNOS, can suppress tumor growth and angiogenesis, yet many aggressive, metastatic cancers exhibit low iNOS expression. The molecular basis for this suppression in metastatic melanoma, a highly lethal malignancy known for its organ-specific metastasis patterns, remains poorly defined. Understanding this silencing mechanism is critical, as it may reveal a key vulnerability exploited by metastatic cells to survive and proliferate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that metastatic melanoma clones selectively silence iNOS expression through promoter hypermethylation and repressive histone modifications, and that reversal of this epigenetic silencing will restore NO-mediated suppression of tumor growth and endothelial cell function in vitro and in vivo.
proposals.method: Using established human melanoma cell lines with varying metastatic potential (e.g., from Dr. Fidler's lab), we will first correlate iNOS mRNA and protein levels with metastatic behavior. We will then perform bisulfite sequencing of the iNOS promoter and chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP) for repressive histone marks (H3K9me3, H3K27me3).
