Functional validation will involve treating low-iNOS metastatic clones with the DNA methyltransferase inhibitor 5-aza-2'-deoxycytidine and/or histone deacetylase inhibitors, followed by assays for iNOS expression, NO production, tumor cell proliferation, and endothelial tube formation in co-culture models.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a specific epigenetic mechanism responsible for iNOS loss in metastatic melanoma, directly linking epigenetic dysregulation to a critical tumor-suppressive pathway. It will provide a rationale for combining epigenetic modulators with immunotherapies or anti-angiogenic agents to reactivate endogenous NO-mediated defense systems against metastasis.
proposals.novelty: While epigenetic silencing of tumor suppressors is known, applying this paradigm specifically to iNOS in the context of organ-specific melanoma metastasis is novel. The approach integrates Dr. Fidler's expertise in metastasis and angiogenesis with contemporary epigenetic analysis to explain a long-observed but poorly understood phenotypic switch in aggressive tumors.

proposals.title: Systemic Analysis of Inducible Nitric Oxide Synthase (iNOS) Expression and Its Inverse Correlation with Metastatic Potential in Human Carcinomas and Murine Models
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies, including seminal work from this lab, established a paradoxical inverse correlation between iNOS expression and metastatic potential in murine melanoma and fibrosarcoma models. This challenges the conventional view of nitric oxide as a pro-angiogenic and pro-metastatic factor. It is critical to determine if this counterintuitive relationship is a general phenomenon across major human cancer types, as it could reveal a novel, endogenous metastasis-suppressive pathway with significant therapeutic implications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high tumoral iNOS expression inversely correlates with metastatic propensity across multiple human carcinoma types (e.g., colon, renal, prostate) and corresponding murine models, and that this effect is mediated through iNOS-derived nitric oxide modulating the expression of key metastasis-regulatory genes in tumor and endothelial cells.
proposals.method: We will utilize human tissue microarrays from primary and metastatic lesions of colon, renal, and prostate carcinomas for immunohistochemical analysis of iNOS protein. iNOS mRNA levels will be quantified via RT-qPCR in matched primary tumor samples with known clinical outcomes. In parallel, we will employ established murine models of colon cancer (CT26) liver metastasis and renal cell carcinoma (RENCA) lung metastasis. Tumor cells will be engineered for stable iNOS overexpression or knockdown using lentiviral vectors. Metastatic burden will be quantified in vivo, and endothelial cell tube formation assays will assess angiogenesis. Gene expression profiling of tumor and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) treated with iNOS-conditioned media will be analyzed using pre-2026 bioinformatics tools (e.g., DAVID, GSEA) to identify conserved pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the generalizability of the iNOS-metastasis suppressor link, moving it from a model-specific observation to a potential oncogenic principle. It will provide a comprehensive molecular signature of iNOS-mediated effects on tumor and stromal compartments. The findings could identify iNOS as a novel prognostic biomarker for metastatic risk and lay the groundwork for therapeutic strategies aimed at activating this endogenous protective pathway.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 research focuses on iNOS as a tumor-promoting enzyme, this proposal directly tests its putative metastasis-suppressive role across diverse cancers. The integrated approach comparing human clinical specimens with isogenic murine models engineered for iNOS manipulation is designed to establish causal mechanism alongside clinical correlation.

proposals.title: Stromal Cell-Mediated Induction of Inducible Nitric Oxide Synthase in Metastatic Tumor Cells: A Determinant of Dormancy and Outgrowth
proposals.motivation: The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a critical regulator of metastatic progression, yet the specific mechanisms by which stromal cells influence the fate of disseminated cancer cells remain incompletely understood. Pre-2026 work has established that inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) and its product, nitric oxide (NO), can exert dual pro- and anti-tumor effects, including inducing growth arrest and apoptosis, which may enforce metastatic dormancy. This proposal directly addresses the gap in understanding how the TME, particularly stromal cells, regulates iNOS in metastatic cells to impact disease recurrence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific stromal cell populations within the metastatic niche, particularly tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) and cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs), can induce iNOS expression and activity in metastatic tumor cells, leading to NO-mediated growth suppression and a shift towards a dormant phenotype.
proposals.method: We will employ a reductionist yet physiologically relevant human cell co-culture system. Human metastatic colon or renal carcinoma cells (pre-2026 lines from Fidler's lab) will be co-cultured with primary human endothelial cells, fibroblasts, or monocyte-derived macrophages. iNOS induction in tumor cells will be quantified via qRT-PCR for mRNA, western blot, and immunofluorescence. Conditioned media and Transwell assays will distinguish contact-dependent from paracrine signaling. The functional consequence of stromal-induced iNOS on tumor cell proliferation, apoptosis, and long-term survival will be assessed using IncuCyte live-cell imaging and clonogenic assays. Key candidate inducing factors (e.g., IFN-α, TNF-α, IL-1β) identified from cytokine arrays will be validated using neutralizing antibodies.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel stromal-tumor cell interaction that may enforce metastatic dormancy, providing a mechanistic link between the inflammatory TME and cancer cell fate. It will identify specific stromal effectors and signaling pathways responsible for iNOS induction, offering potential therapeutic targets to either suppress awakening of dormant cells or therapeutically induce dormancy.
proposals.novelty: While iNOS expression in tumors is known, the explicit interrogation of stromal cells as the *inducers* of iNOS specifically in metastatic cells to alter their fate (dormancy vs. outgrowth) is a novel and non-obvious extension of the 'seed and soil' hypothesis, moving beyond correlative studies to mechanistic, cell-cell interaction experiments.

proposals.title: Host Macrophage iNOS Expression Dictates the Organ-Specific Metastatic Efficiency of Murine Melanoma
proposals.motivation: The role of nitric oxide (NO) in cancer is paradoxical, with evidence for both pro- and anti-tumorigenic effects. This duality is particularly relevant in the tumor microenvironment, where inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) in host stromal cells, especially macrophages, is a major source of NO. Pre-2026 work by Fidler's group and others has established the critical influence of the organ microenvironment ('seed and soil') and stromal factors like angiogenesis on metastasis, but the specific, context-dependent function of macrophage-derived iNOS in regulating the formation of melanoma metastases remains unresolved and is a key gap in understanding stromal regulation of metastasis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that iNOS expression in host macrophages exerts a dual, organ-specific effect on melanoma metastasis, promoting metastatic seeding and growth in some organ microenvironments while inhibiting it in others, mediated through differential effects on tumor cell survival, angiogenesis, and immune modulation.
proposals.method: We will employ a well-established murine B16 melanoma metastasis model in iNOS knockout (iNOS-/-) and wild-type (WT) C57BL/6 mice. Bone marrow-derived macrophages (BMDMs) from iNOS-/- and WT mice will be characterized for polarization states (M1/M2) using flow cytometry for surface markers (CD80, CD206) and mRNA analysis of cytokine profiles. For in vivo studies, we will generate chimeric mice by reconstituting lethally irradiated WT mice with iNOS-/- or WT bone marrow, allowing us to isolate the role of hematopoietic (macrophage) iNOS. Metastatic burden will be quantified in target organs (lung, liver) following intravenous or intrasplenic injection. We will analyze metastatic foci for apoptosis (TUNEL), proliferation (Ki67), angiogenesis (CD31 immunohistochemistry), and macrophage infiltration (F4/80). Systemic and intra-tumoral NO levels will be measured via nitrite/nitrate assay and DAF-FM DA staining.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively resolve the conflicting literature on iNOS in metastasis by delineating its organ-specific functions. It will provide a mechanistic framework linking macrophage polarization, NO production, and key metastatic processes (angiogenesis, survival) within distinct organ microenvironments. The findings could identify macrophage iNOS as a predictive biomarker or a context-dependent therapeutic target for metastatic melanoma.
proposals.novelty: While iNOS has been studied in cancer, this proposal uniquely focuses on its expression specifically in the host macrophage compartment and tests its functional impact across different organ microenvironments of metastasis, moving beyond a binary pro- vs. anti-tumor assessment to a nuanced, site-specific understanding.

proposals.title: Correlation of Angiogenic and Metastatic Gene Expression Signatures Between Primary Tumors and Spontaneous Metastases in an Orthotopic Renal Cell Carcinoma Model
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, most experimental metastasis studies rely on intravenous injection of tumor cells, bypassing critical steps of the metastatic cascade. This limits understanding of the molecular evolution from primary tumor to distant site. Dr. Fidler's prior work correlating gene expression with pathological stage underscores the need to validate such correlations in a more physiologically relevant, spontaneous metastasis model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the expression levels of key genes regulating angiogenesis (e.g., VEGF, PDGF) and metastasis (e.g., MMPs, chemokine receptors) in orthotopic primary renal cell carcinomas will directly correlate with their expression in matched spontaneous lung and liver metastases, and that this paired signature will be a stronger predictor of outcome than the primary tumor signature alone.
proposals.method: We will establish orthotopic primary human renal cell carcinoma (RCC) tumors in nude mice using patient-derived or well-characterized cell lines (e.g., 786-O). Animals will be monitored until signs of metastasis develop. Primary tumors and matched spontaneous metastases will be harvested. RNA will be extracted and analyzed via quantitative RT-PCR and Nanostring nCounter assays for a custom panel of genes implicated in angiogenesis, metastasis, and immune modulation, based on Dr. Fidler's previous publications. Correlation analysis (Pearson/Spearman) will be performed between primary and metastatic expression profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, paired molecular comparison between primary tumors and their spontaneous metastases within the same host, a dataset largely missing from pre-2026 literature. It will validate whether primary tumor expression profiles are reliable surrogates for metastatic disease biology. The findings could refine prognostic biomarkers and identify conserved therapeutic targets across both sites.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, gene-expression-focused analysis of spontaneously derived metastases from orthotopic primaries, moving beyond injection-based models. This directly tests the fidelity of the primary tumor as a molecular mirror of its metastases in a controlled experimental setting that recapitulates the full metastatic cascade.

proposals.title: Functional Redundancy in Tumor Angiogenesis: Assessing the Role of IL-8 Knockdown in Vascularization of Human Colon Cancer Liver Metastases
proposals.motivation: Targeting single angiogenic factors like VEGF has shown limited efficacy, suggesting redundancy in angiogenic pathways. IL-8 is a potent chemokine implicated in tumor angiogenesis and metastasis, particularly in cancers like renal cell carcinoma. Determining if IL-8 is a primary, non-redundant driver is critical for designing effective anti-angiogenic therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific knockdown of IL-8 in human colon carcinoma cells will fail to significantly reduce vascularization and growth of liver metastases in vivo, indicating functional redundancy among angiogenic factors.
proposals.method: We will use lentiviral-mediated shRNA to stably knockdown IL-8 expression in human colon cancer cell lines (e.g., KM12SM). Knockdown efficiency will be validated by qRT-PCR for mRNA and ELISA for protein. These cells will be injected into the spleens of nude mice to establish liver metastases. Vascularization will be quantified by immunohistochemistry for CD31 in harvested lesions, and metastasis burden will be measured. Parallel in vitro assays will assess human endothelial cell tube formation using conditioned media from knockdown cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the functional importance of IL-8 in a clinically relevant organ-specific metastasis model. It will clarify whether anti-IL-8 monotherapy is a viable strategy or if combinatorial targeting is necessary. The findings will inform the development of more effective angiogenesis suppression protocols for metastatic disease.
proposals.novelty: While IL-8 expression correlates with advanced disease, its functional necessity as a primary driver, versus a redundant component, in the complex angiogenic network of established liver metastases remains untested. This study moves beyond correlation to direct causal testing in an organ-specific microenvironment.

proposals.title: Intravital Microscopy Reveals IL-8-Mediated Dynamic Vascular Dysfunction in Human Melanoma Brain Metastasis Models
proposals.motivation: The chemokine IL-8 is a potent promoter of tumor angiogenesis and metastasis, but its real-time effects on tumor vessel formation, permeability, and blood flow dynamics remain poorly characterized. This gap is critical because dysfunctional tumor vasculature drives hypoxia, immune evasion, and therapeutic resistance, particularly in hard-to-treat sites like the brain. Dr. Fidler's prior work on angiogenesis and metastasis in renal and colon cancer provides a strong foundation for this investigation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tumor-derived IL-8 drives the formation of structurally and functionally abnormal tumor vasculature, characterized by excessive sprouting, increased permeability, and chaotic blood flow, which can be directly visualized and quantified using intravital microscopy.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution, multi-photon intravital microscopy (IVM) in established human melanoma brain metastasis xenograft models in nude mice. Tumor cells will be engineered to overexpress or knockdown IL-8 via lentiviral vectors. Mice will be implanted with cranial windows for longitudinal imaging. We will use fluorescent dextrans and lectins to label blood plasma and vessels, respectively, and track real-time vascular parameters. Image analysis will utilize established algorithms (e.g., in ImageJ/FIJI) for automated quantification of vessel density, diameter, permeability (extravasation rate), and red blood cell velocity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, dynamic visualization of IL-8's role in shaping tumor vascular architecture and function in vivo. It will establish quantitative, functional benchmarks for vascular abnormality driven by a key metastatic chemokine. The findings could identify IL-8-mediated vascular phenotypes as biomarkers or therapeutic targets to normalize tumor blood flow and improve drug delivery.
proposals.novelty: While IL-8's pro-angiogenic role is known from static assays, applying IVM for dynamic, functional analysis of its effects in a clinically relevant brain metastasis model is novel. This approach moves beyond correlative gene expression studies to establish direct causative links between IL-8 signaling and dysfunctional vascular physiology.

proposals.title: Cell-Specific Quantification of IL-8 Expression in Human Colon Cancer Liver Metastases Using Laser Capture Microdissection: Implications for Stromal-Tumor Interactions and Anti-Angiogenic Therapy
proposals.motivation: The cytokine IL-8 is a potent promoter of tumor angiogenesis and progression, but its cellular source within the tumor microenvironment remains ambiguous. Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Fidler's work on interferon-alpha and angiogenesis in colon cancer metastases, highlight the therapeutic importance of the tumor-stroma interface. Precisely defining which cell populations—malignant epithelial cells or stromal cells (e.g., endothelial cells, macrophages)—are the dominant producers of IL-8 in clinical metastases is critical for understanding resistance to anti-angiogenic therapies and developing targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within human colon cancer liver metastases, the tumor-associated stromal compartment, not the malignant epithelial cells, is the predominant source of IL-8 expression, and that high stromal IL-8 correlates with increased microvessel density and poor response to systemic therapy.
proposals.method: We will apply laser capture microdissection (LCM) to frozen sections of human colon cancer liver metastases obtained from a pre-existing, clinically annotated tissue bank. Pure populations of malignant epithelial cells and adjacent stromal cells (including endothelial cells) will be isolated. RNA will be extracted from these captured cells, and IL-8 mRNA expression will be quantified using real-time quantitative RT-PCR (TaqMan assays). Expression levels will be normalized to housekeeping genes (GAPDH, ACTB) and correlated with clinical outcomes (progression-free/overall survival) and immunohistochemical markers of angiogenesis (CD31) and phosphorylated PDGF receptor status from prior studies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative evidence of cell-specific IL-8 contribution in clinical specimens of colon cancer metastasis. It will clarify the cellular drivers of angiogenesis in this setting and may identify stromal IL-8 as a biomarker for patient stratification. The findings will directly inform combination therapies targeting the stromal compartment, building on Dr. Fidler's foundational work with interferon-alpha.
proposals.novelty: While LCM and IL-8 measurement are established techniques, their combined application to cleanly separate and compare tumor versus stromal expression in clinical metastasis samples is novel. This moves beyond bulk tissue analysis or in vitro models to deliver a precise, spatially resolved molecular profile directly relevant to the in vivo therapeutic challenge.

proposals.title: Quantifying IL-8-Mediated Endothelial-Tumor Cell Crosstalk in a 3D Co-Culture Model to Predict Metastatic Progression
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Fidler's own work, established that tumor-derived cytokines like IL-8 are critical for angiogenesis and metastasis, but the dynamic, reciprocal signaling between endothelial cells and tumor cells in a physiologically relevant 3D microenvironment remains poorly characterized. Current 2D assays fail to capture the complex spatial interactions that govern vessel sprouting and tumor cell intravasation, a key bottleneck in understanding metastatic efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that IL-8 secretion by specific human carcinoma cells in a 3D co-culture system will induce a quantifiable pro-angiogenic phenotype in human endothelial cells, and that this endothelial activation will, in turn, enhance tumor cell invasive properties, creating a feed-forward loop predictive of metastatic potential.
proposals.method: We will employ a standardized 3D fibrin gel bead angiogenesis assay. Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) will be co-cultured with GFP-labeled human renal cell carcinoma (RCC) or colon carcinoma cell lines with known metastatic potential. Using siRNA-mediated knockdown and recombinant protein supplementation, we will modulate IL-8 expression. Sprout formation (number, length) will be quantified via confocal microscopy and automated image analysis using AngioTool software. Concurrently, we will measure endothelial and tumor cell gene expression changes via qRT-PCR for metastasis-regulating genes (e.g., MMPs, VEGF) and secreted IL-8 via ELISA.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic link between IL-8 signaling, 3D angiogenic sprouting, and tumor cell phenotypic changes, moving beyond correlation to causation. It will establish a functional in vitro assay to rank the pro-metastatic capacity of tumor cells based on their ability to dysregulate endothelial biology, offering a potential platform for testing anti-angiogenic/anti-metastatic agents like interferon-alpha.
proposals.novelty: While 3D angiogenesis assays exist, their systematic application to dissect the bidirectional signaling of a specific cytokine (IL-8) within a tumor-endothelial co-culture, with parallel functional and molecular readouts, is novel and directly extends Dr. Fidler's legacy in linking angiogenesis regulators to metastasis.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Modeling of IL-8 Diffusion to Predict Angiogenic Hotspots and Metastatic Potential in Human Carcinoma
proposals.motivation: Angiogenesis, driven by cytokine gradients, is critical for tumor progression and metastasis, as demonstrated by Dr. Fidler's work on interferon-alpha and renal cell carcinoma gene expression. However, the precise spatial dynamics of key chemokines like IL-8, a potent endothelial cell mitogen and angiogenic factor, remain poorly characterized. A computational model defining IL-8 concentration gradients from tumor cells to surrounding stroma would provide a mechanistic framework to understand heterogeneous vessel recruitment and identify targets for anti-angiogenic therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that computational modeling of IL-8 diffusion from heterogeneous tumor cell clusters will reveal stable spatial concentration gradients that correlate with in vitro and in vivo patterns of endothelial cell migration and microvessel density, predicting sites of aggressive local growth and metastatic propensity.
proposals.method: We will develop a reaction-diffusion model using partial differential equations (PDEs) implemented in MATLAB or COMSOL Multiphysics. The model will incorporate parameters for IL-8 secretion rates (derived from mRNA expression data in human cancer cell lines), diffusion coefficients in extracellular matrix, and binding/decay kinetics. Tumor geometry will be informed by histology from patient-derived xenografts of colon cancer and renal cell carcinoma. Model-predicted gradient maps will be validated using transwell migration assays of human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and by correlating gradient profiles with CD31-stained vessel patterns in matched tissue sections.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, spatially explicit link between tumor cell cytokine expression and the resulting angiogenic microenvironment. It will provide a predictive tool to identify tumors most reliant on IL-8-driven angiogenesis, potentially refining patient selection for therapies targeting this axis. The model will be a novel platform to test 'gradient disruption' as an anti-angiogenic strategy.
proposals.novelty: While cytokine biology is well-studied, applying rigorous computational diffusion modeling specifically to IL-8 gradients from discrete tumor foci to guide vessel growth is novel. This approach moves beyond measuring bulk expression levels to mechanistically connect tumor heterogeneity with the spatial architecture of its vasculature.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Monitoring of IL-8 and Dynamic Contrast-Enhanced MRI Parameters as Predictive Biomarkers of Therapy Response and Metastatic Progression in Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Fidler's, established that interleukin-8 (IL-8) is a potent promoter of tumor angiogenesis, metastasis, and chemoresistance. However, its role as a dynamic, systemic biomarker during therapy is poorly characterized. Simultaneously, non-invasive imaging of tumor vasculature via DCE-MRI provides a direct readout of the angiogenic phenotype. Correlating these systemic and local measures over time could reveal critical windows of therapeutic vulnerability and mechanisms of resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal changes in circulating IL-8 levels will correlate with quantitative changes in tumor vascular parameters (e.g., Ktrans, kep from DCE-MRI) during therapy, and that their combined trajectory will predict clinical outcomes (progression-free survival, development of new metastases) more accurately than baseline measurements alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study in patients with metastatic renal cell carcinoma or colorectal cancer liver metastases undergoing systemic therapy (e.g., anti-angiogenic agents, interferon-alpha). Serial blood draws will be performed at baseline, during cycles, and at progression for quantification of IL-8 via ELISA and analysis of mRNA expression in peripheral blood leukocytes. Patients will undergo synchronized DCE-MRI scans at the same timepoints. We will use linear mixed-effects models to analyze longitudinal trajectories and Cox proportional hazards regression with time-dependent covariates to assess the predictive power of IL-8 and MRI vascular parameters for progression-free and overall survival.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a novel, integrated biomarker panel (systemic cytokine + local imaging) for real-time monitoring of therapy-induced vascular changes and tumor-host interactions. It will provide mechanistic insight into how therapy modulates the angiogenic microenvironment and identify early indicators of treatment failure, enabling potential therapeutic adjustments.
proposals.novelty: While IL-8 and DCE-MRI have been studied separately, their synchronous, longitudinal correlation in patients during active therapy is novel. This approach moves beyond static biomarker assessment to a dynamic model that captures the evolving biology of metastasis and treatment response, directly extending Dr. Fidler's foundational work on angiogenesis and systemic therapy.

proposals.title: Clonal Stability versus Microenvironmental Modulation: Defining the Determinants of Metastatic Phenotype in Human Carcinoma Models
proposals.motivation: The seminal work of Fidler and others established that tumors are heterogeneous and contain subpopulations with differing metastatic potential. However, a critical unresolved question is whether this metastatic phenotype, once selected for, is a fixed, intrinsic property of a cancer clone, or whether it can be dynamically regulated by extrinsic signals from the tumor microenvironment (TME). Resolving this is essential for understanding metastatic progression and for designing therapies that target either stable clone vulnerabilities or malleable TME interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the metastatic potential of agar-selected tumor clones is a stable, intrinsic property that is not fundamentally altered by co-culture with specific stromal components of the TME, such as endothelial cells or fibroblasts, which may modulate growth but not core metastatic competence.
proposals.method: We will isolate high- and low-metastatic clones from human renal cell carcinoma (RCC) lines via agar selection, a pre-2026 method pioneered by Fidler. These clones will be co-cultured (direct and indirect) with primary human endothelial cells or cancer-associated fibroblasts. Post-co-culture, we will assess functional metastatic traits: in vitro invasion (Matrigel), angiogenesis (endothelial tube formation assay), and in vivo metastatic potential in nude mice via tail vein (lung colonization) and orthotopic (spontaneous metastasis) assays. We will analyze pre- and post-co-culture clones using mRNA expression profiling (microarray) focused on Fidler-relevant metastasis-regulating genes (e.g., MMPs, VEGF, integrins) and angiogenic factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the 'clonal dominance' versus 'phenotypic plasticity' models of metastasis. It will identify whether specific TME components can reprogram metastatic fate, with implications for targeting stable clone properties versus TME-mediated signaling. The gene expression data will provide a signature of stable metastatic determinants versus microenvironment-responsive pathways.
proposals.novelty: While prior work correlates gene expression with stage or studies TME effects on bulk tumors, this proposal uniquely isolates stable clones and systematically challenges their fixed phenotype with defined stromal elements, a direct experimental test of a foundational concept in metastasis biology.

proposals.title: Discordance between anchorage-independent growth in agar and metastatic potential: A functional and transcriptomic validation of the soft agar assay.
proposals.motivation: The soft agar colony formation assay is a cornerstone in vitro method for identifying tumorigenic and potentially metastatic cells, based on the principle of anchorage-independent growth. However, its absolute fidelity in selecting for cells capable of completing the metastatic cascade in vivo remains an unresolved, critical assumption. Validating or refuting this fidelity is essential for interpreting decades of research and for improving preclinical models of metastasis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the soft agar assay is an imperfect surrogate for metastatic potential, and that discordant populations exist: (1) cells that form colonies in agar but are non-metastatic in vivo, and (2) metastatic cells that fail to grow in agar.
proposals.method: Using well-characterized human carcinoma cell lines (e.g., from colon or renal cell carcinoma), we will isolate single-cell-derived clones. Each clone will be tested in standard soft agar assays. We will then perform in vivo metastasis assays in immunodeficient mice via intracardiac or intrasplenic injection, comparing clones that are agar-positive/negative. Finally, we will use RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) to define the differential gene expression profiles, focusing on metastasis-regulating and angiogenesis-related genes (e.g., from Dr. Fidler's prior work), between the four phenotypic groups (agar+/met+, agar+/met-, agar-/met+, agar-/met-).
proposals.contribution: This work will directly quantify the predictive accuracy of the soft agar assay for metastatic outcome. It will identify specific transcriptional programs associated with the divergent phenotypes, offering mechanistic insight into biological processes that are required for agar growth versus in vivo metastasis. This will refine the use of this classic assay in metastasis research.
proposals.novelty: While the agar assay is widely used, a systematic, clonal-level comparison of its results against definitive in vivo metastatic potential, coupled with deep molecular profiling of the discordant populations, has not been comprehensively performed. This directly tests a fundamental assumption in cancer biology.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Single-Cell Functional Profiling of Metastatic Potential via Integrated mRNA Sequencing and Microfluidic Invasion Assays
proposals.motivation: Metastasis is the primary cause of cancer mortality, driven by heterogeneous subpopulations of cells with varying metastatic potential. Current bulk-tumor analyses obscure this critical heterogeneity, and existing single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) provides only correlative molecular signatures, not functional validation of invasive and angiogenic capacity. A direct, high-throughput functional assay at single-cell resolution is needed to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating high-content, single-cell functional assays of invasion and endothelial tube disruption with parallel scRNA-seq will identify and validate a conserved, functionally verified gene expression signature predictive of metastatic potential across solid tumors.
proposals.method: We will establish a pipeline using patient-derived organoids from renal cell and colon carcinomas. Single cells will be loaded into a custom microfluidic device (CellASIC ONIX2) featuring parallel channels for Matrigel invasion and co-culture with human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) to assess disruption. High-content imaging will quantify invasion and angiogenic disruption per cell. Immediately post-assay, individual cells from functional channels will be indexed and captured for scRNA-seq using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform. Data integration and signature discovery will be performed using Seurat for clustering and Monocle3 for trajectory analysis, correlating functional phenotypes with gene expression modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first directly linked atlas of single-cell metastatic function and transcriptome, moving beyond correlative signatures. It will deliver a validated, functionally defined gene set for prognostic stratification and identify novel, targetable pathways driving invasion and angiogenesis at the single-cell level.
proposals.novelty: While scRNA-seq and microfluidic invasion assays exist separately, their direct, high-throughput integration on a cell-by-cell basis to empirically link phenotype to genotype for metastasis is novel. This approach functionally validates transcriptional programs of metastasis in a single experimental system.

proposals.title: The Primary Tumor as a Metastatic Niche: Selective Pressure for Aggressive Subclones in Human Renal Cell Carcinoma
proposals.motivation: The 'seed and soil' hypothesis, pioneered by Dr. Fidler, posits that metastasis requires compatible interactions between tumor cells and specific organ microenvironments. However, the primary tumor microenvironment itself is often considered a uniform entity of origin. Recent evidence suggests intra-tumoral heterogeneity, but whether the primary site acts as a selective 'soil' for specific, potentially more aggressive subclones that could later dominate metastatic outgrowth remains unexplored. This concept reframes the primary tumor not just as a source, but as a critical, initial selective site in the metastatic cascade.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary renal cell carcinoma (RCC) microenvironment exerts selective pressure, enriching for subclones with enhanced angiogenic and metastatic gene expression profiles, and that these subclones are primed for survival and growth in distant organ sites.
proposals.method: We will use laser capture microdissection to isolate distinct histological regions (e.g., tumor center, invasive front, perivascular niche) from human primary RCC specimens. From these, we will perform bulk RNA sequencing and single-cell RNA-seq (10x Genomics platform) to profile subclone-specific gene expression, focusing on angiogenesis (VEGF, PDGF) and metastasis-regulating genes. Subclones will be defined by inferred copy number variation and phylogenetic analysis using tools like Monocle3 and InferCNV. In parallel, we will establish matched patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models from these microdissected regions to functionally test their metastatic potential and angiogenic dependency using systemic interferon-alpha therapy as an anti-angiogenic challenge.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the novel concept of the primary tumor as a selective metastatic niche. It will identify the specific gene expression signatures and microenvironmental factors within the primary tumor that favor the expansion of aggressive subclones. The findings will provide a mechanistic link between intra-tumoral heterogeneity observed at diagnosis and subsequent metastatic organotropism, offering potential early biomarkers for progression.
proposals.novelty: While intra-tumoral heterogeneity is recognized, this proposal uniquely inverts the classic metastatic paradigm by investigating the primary site as a selective 'soil,' not just a 'seed' source. It integrates spatial transcriptomics of human tumors with functional PDX validation, a direct extension of Dr. Fidler's foundational 'seed and soil' and angiogenesis therapy work into the era of single-cell and spatial genomics.

proposals.title: Single-Circulating Tumor Cell EGFR Profiling Predicts Metastatic Potential in Colon Cancer
proposals.motivation: Colon cancer mortality is driven by metastasis, often seeded by circulating tumor cells (CTCs). While bulk tumor EGFR expression is a known prognostic factor, CTCs are highly heterogeneous. Pre-2026 technologies now allow isolation and analysis of single CTCs, but the functional status of EGFR in individual CTCs—a potential determinant of their metastatic competency—remains uncharacterized and could be a superior biomarker.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the level and phosphorylation status (i.e., functional activation) of EGFR in individual CTCs from colon cancer patients correlates with and predicts the development of liver metastasis.
proposals.method: CTCs will be isolated from patient blood samples using the CellSearch system or microfluidic enrichment (e.g., CTC-iChip). Individual CTCs will be captured via micromanipulation or DEPArray. For each cell, we will perform multiplexed mRNA analysis using a pre-amplified qRT-PCR panel for EGFR, its ligands (e.g., EGF, TGF-α), and downstream effectors (e.g., VEGFA, MMPs). Parallelly, immunofluorescence staining for total and phosphorylated EGFR (pY1068) will be quantified via high-resolution imaging and computational image analysis (using CellProfiler software) to assess protein level and activation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel single-CTC functional proteogenomic signature for EGFR pathway activation. It will directly test the 'seed' potential of individual CTCs, moving beyond enumeration. The findings could lead to a liquid biopsy-based predictive assay for metastasis risk, enabling earlier therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: Previous work by Fidler and others correlated bulk gene expression with metastasis. This proposal shifts the focus to the functional state of a key oncogenic pathway within the rare, critical metastatic precursors themselves, leveraging single-cell resolution not previously applied to CTC EGFR signaling in colon cancer.

proposals.title: Characterization of Low-EGFR Metastatic Clones in Colon Carcinoma: Mechanisms of Liver Metastasis and Resistance to Anti-Angiogenic Therapy
proposals.motivation: EGFR is a major therapeutic target in colon cancer, yet a subset of patients with low-EGFR tumors still develop lethal metastases, a phenomenon poorly understood. This inverse problem challenges the EGFR-centric paradigm of colon cancer progression. Elucidating the mechanisms driving metastasis in low-EGFR contexts is critical to understanding therapeutic failure and identifying new vulnerabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare colon carcinoma clones with low EGFR expression and function metastasize by activating compensatory pro-metastatic and pro-angiogenic pathways, such as PDGF or interferon-related signaling, which confer resistance to conventional anti-EGFR and anti-VEGF therapies.
proposals.method: We will isolate rare low-EGFR human colon carcinoma cells from established cell lines (e.g., HT-29, HCT-116) using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS). Their metastatic potential will be validated in a nude mouse model of splenic injection/liver metastasis. mRNA expression profiling (RNA-seq) of sorted low-EGFR vs. high-EGFR metastatic cells will identify differentially expressed genes regulating metastasis and angiogenesis, with focus on pathways from Fidler's prior work (PDGFR, interferon-alpha). Functional validation will use siRNA knockdown of top candidate genes in vivo.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the molecular signature and functional drivers of an understudied, therapy-resistant metastatic cell population. It will provide a mechanistic explanation for metastasis without high EGFR, potentially identifying new biomarkers (e.g., phosphorylated PDGFR in circulating cells) and therapeutic targets for patients who fail standard biologic therapies.
proposals.novelty: The search is focused on the 'inverse' or outlier population that contradicts the established EGFR-high metastasis model, a deliberate inversion of the typical selection strategy. Integrating Fidler's expertise in metastasis, angiogenesis, and systemic cytokine therapy (IFN-α) to study low-EGFR cells is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Patient-Derived Colon Cancer Organoids as a Model for EGFR-Mediated Invasion and Metastatic Niche Formation
proposals.motivation: Metastasis remains the primary cause of mortality in colon cancer, yet studying the complex, multi-step process of invasion and distant niche establishment in vivo is challenging. Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Fidler's own studies on angiogenesis and therapy in colon cancer liver metastasis, highlights the need for more physiologically relevant, human-specific models. Patient-derived organoids (PDOs) have emerged as a powerful tool to recapitulate tumor heterogeneity and architecture, but their application to systematically dissect the molecular drivers of invasion and pre-metastatic niche formation is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that EGFR signaling in patient-derived colon cancer organoids drives a transcriptional program that promotes invasive behavior and the secretion of factors which remodel the microenvironment to facilitate metastasis.
proposals.method: We will establish a biobank of colon cancer PDOs from primary and metastatic sites. Using a miniaturized 3D invasion assay in Matrigel, we will quantify organoid invasive potential. We will treat organoids with EGFR inhibitors (e.g., cetuximab) and use bulk mRNA sequencing to identify differentially expressed genes related to invasion, angiogenesis (e.g., VEGF, PDGF), and immune modulation. Secretome analysis via cytokine arrays will profile factors released into conditioned media. To model the metastatic niche, we will co-culture endothelial cell spheroids with invasive organoid-conditioned media and assess tube formation and gene expression (e.g., via qPCR for ICAM-1, VCAM-1).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, scalable link between patient-specific EGFR signaling, invasive phenotype, and pro-metastatic microenvironment remodeling in a human 3D system. It will identify a candidate gene signature and secreted factor profile predictive of aggressive disease, providing a platform for personalized drug testing. The findings will advance the mechanistic understanding of metastasis initiated by Fidler's 'seed and soil' hypothesis.
proposals.novelty: While organoids are used for drug screening, their systematic application to model the functional cascade from oncogenic signaling (EGFR) to invasive behavior and subsequent endothelial activation—key steps in metastasis—within a single, patient-specific platform is novel. This integrates Fidler's expertise in metastasis and angiogenesis with cutting-edge pre-2026 organoid technology.

proposals.title: A Tri-Cellular 3D Collagen Matrix Model of Colon Cancer Liver Metastasis Incorporating Carcinoma-Associated Fibroblasts and Tumor-Associated Macrophages to Study Stromal-Driven Angiogenesis and Therapy Response
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Fidler's own work, established that the tumor microenvironment (TME), particularly stromal cells like fibroblasts and immune cells, critically regulates metastasis, angiogenesis, and therapy resistance. Existing 3D collagen models of cancer masses often focus on tumor and endothelial cells, failing to capture the full complexity of stromal interactions. A more physiologically relevant model is needed to dissect how specific stromal cell types modulate tumor progression and response to agents like interferon-alpha.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating carcinoma-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) and tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) into a 3D collagen-based model of human colon cancer cells and human endothelial cells will create a more predictive system, where stromal cells will enhance pro-angiogenic factor expression and confer resistance to anti-angiogenic therapy.
proposals.method: We will establish a 3D collagen type I matrix co-culturing HT-29 human colon carcinoma cells, human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), and primary human CAFs and monocyte-derived TAMs. Using quantitative RT-PCR and multiplex ELISA, we will measure the expression of key angiogenesis (VEGF, bFGF) and metastasis-regulating genes (as in Fidler's RCC paper) in mono- and co-cultures. The model will be treated with systemic interferon-alpha, and therapy response will be assessed by measuring endothelial network formation, stromal cell viability via flow cytometry, and changes in phosphorylated PDGF receptor signaling in extracted leukocytes, mirroring Fidler's prior methodology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, tractable ex vivo platform to dissect the specific contributions of CAFs and TAMs to angiogenesis and therapy resistance in metastatic colon cancer. It will directly extend Dr. Fidler's legacy in metastasis research by adding critical stromal complexity to his established models, offering new insights into combination therapies targeting the stromal compartment.
proposals.novelty: While 3D co-cultures exist, the specific integration of CAFs and TAMs with carcinoma and endothelial cells in a single collagen matrix to systematically quantify their combined impact on angiogenic gene expression and response to a clinically relevant cytokine (IFN-α) is novel and builds directly on Fidler's unique expertise in metastasis models and angiogenesis therapy.

ideas.title: Functionally isolate and validate the acidosis-responsive cis-element and trans-factor for IL-8 mRNA stabilization using synthetic reporters and orthogonal perturbation.
ideas.description: This study employs a dual synthetic biology and functional genomics strategy to definitively identify the mechanism of IL-8 mRNA stabilization in acidosis. First, candidate cis-elements from the IL-8 3'UTR are inserted into a chimeric, destabilized reporter mRNA to test their sufficiency for conferring acidosis-induced stability in a controlled context. Second, a CRISPR interference (CRISPRi) screen targeting known and predicted RNA-binding proteins is conducted in parallel to identify trans-factors whose knockdown specifically abrogates the acidosis-induced stabilization of both the endogenous IL-8 mRNA and the synthetic reporter. This combined approach disentangles specific regulation from global effects, establishing direct causal relationships for the identified elements and proteins.

ideas.title: pH-dependent post-transcriptional network modeling of cytokine mRNA stability
ideas.description: This project integrates experimental RNA-protein interactome data from pH-modulated pulldown assays directly into a computational network model. Biotinylated IL-8 mRNA pulldowns under acidic versus normal pH conditions will generate a dynamic dataset of protein-binding partners, which serves as the foundational, biologically parameterized input for a systems biology model of the cytokine mRNA stability network. The computational model will then simulate network behavior, predicting how perturbations (e.g., specific protein knockdowns, further pH changes) alter IL-8 mRNA half-life and output, which can be validated experimentally. This closed loop between context-specific experimental interactomics and predictive network modeling enables the discovery of key pH-sensitive regulatory nodes and offers a generalizable framework for understanding post-transcriptional control in the tumor microenvironment.

ideas.title: Comparative 3D chromatin architecture of the IL-8 locus across endothelial cell types under acidosis
ideas.description: This project will apply chromatin conformation capture (3C/Hi-C) methods to systematically compare how acidosis remodels the 3D architecture of the IL-8 promoter and its enhancer interactions in macrovascular (e.g., HUVEC), microvascular, and arterial endothelial cells. By integrating the architectural analysis across distinct endothelial subtypes, it will test the hypothesis that cell-type-specific vulnerability or resistance to acidosis-driven inflammation is encoded in divergent chromatin folding landscapes and long-range regulatory circuits. This approach can reveal if the 'step-specific' transcriptional regulation is conserved at the structural level or if different endothelial beds employ distinct architectural strategies to control IL-8. The outcome will be a mechanistic map linking extracellular acidosis to cell-type-specific 3D genome reorganization and inflammatory output.

ideas.title: Genetic background dictates the interdependence of angiogenic steps in ovarian cancer
ideas.description: This study investigates whether the genetic background of ovarian cancer cell lines determines the degree of coupling between distinct steps of angiogenesis, such as endothelial cell migration and tube formation. We will modulate one step (e.g., using a migration inhibitor) in isogenic or diverse ovarian cancer cell lines with defined mutations (e.g., BRCA, p53, KRAS) and measure the downstream impact on other steps. This approach simultaneously challenges the generality of IL-8's role and tests if the independence of angiogenic processes is itself a cell-line-specific trait. The outcome will identify specific genetic contexts where angiogenic programs are functionally monolithic versus modular, offering new targets for context-specific anti-angiogenic therapy.

ideas.title: Tumor-specific endothelial coupling: Testing if angiogenic step interdependence is dictated by a unique cytokine hierarchy
ideas.description: This research direction directly tests the hypothesis that the interdependence of angiogenic steps (e.g., migration and tube formation) is not a fixed biological rule but is instead dictated by the specific cytokine hierarchy present in the tumor microenvironment. We will isolate primary tumor-derived endothelial cells (TECs) from various cancers and compare them to HUVECs and normal tissue endothelial cells. By systematically modulating one step (e.g., using a migration inhibitor) and measuring the downstream effect on another step (e.g., tube formation), we will map the coupling strength between steps. Crucially, we will correlate this coupling map with the cells' unique cytokine response profiles to identify if tumor-specific cytokine hierarchies rewire the core logic of the angiogenic cascade.

ideas.title: Temporal mapping of pH-dependent mRNA-protein interactomes for cytokine signaling sequences
ideas.description: This project investigates the hypothesis that the functional outcome of sequential cytokine exposure is dictated by pH-dependent remodeling of specific mRNA-protein interactomes, not merely by cytokine presence. We will use biotinylated mRNA pulldowns for key cytokine-induced transcripts (like IL-8) to capture their binding proteomes at precise timepoints during defined cytokine sequences, comparing conditions of normal versus acidic extracellular pH. This approach will identify how the timing of cytokine signals reprograms the post-transcriptional regulatory machinery in a pH-sensitive manner, directly testing if specific protein-binding events on target mRNAs are the required effectors of temporal cytokine codes.

ideas.title: Systematic Transcription Factor Discovery for Acidosis-Induced IL-8 via a Protein-DNA Interaction Screening Approach
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that AP-1 and NF-κB are the primary or sole mediators of acidosis-induced IL-8 expression by proposing a systematic, unbiased discovery approach adapted from plant abiotic stress research. In plant biology, to identify novel regulators of stress-responsive genes, researchers use protein-DNA interaction screens (e.g., Y1H, PBMs) against the gene's promoter. We adapt this solution by screening a comprehensive library of human transcription factor proteins for their ability to bind the IL-8 promoter region under acidic (pH ~6.5) versus normal (pH ~7.4) conditions in vitro. This direct biochemical screen would identify all TFs whose DNA-binding affinity for the IL-8 promoter is pH-sensitive, potentially revealing novel acidosis-responsive pathways independent of AP-1/NF-κB.

ideas.title: Adapt a plant hypoxia mRNA stability screen to identify acidosis-responsive IL-8 mRNA stabilizers
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from plant stress biology—the use of comparative RNA interactome capture (RIC) to identify condition-specific RNA-binding proteins (RBPs)—to solve the analogous problem in immunology/cancer biology. In plant hypoxia studies, RIC compared RBPs bound to mRNA in normal versus low-oxygen conditions to find stabilizers of anaerobic response mRNAs. Adapted here, we propose applying comparative RIC to human cells (e.g., cancer or immune cells) under physiological versus acidic pH conditions. The mass spectrometry analysis of captured RBPs will create an unbiased map of the acidosis-responsive mRNA interactome, directly identifying candidate RBPs that bind to and likely stabilize IL-8 mRNA. Subsequent validation would involve mapping the specific cis-elements in the IL-8 3'UTR that recruit these acidosis-induced RBPs, thereby elucidating the core mechanism of IL-8 mRNA stabilization in acidosis.

ideas.title: Causal dissection of pH dynamics in IL-8 induction via integrated biosensor-actuator systems
ideas.description: This study employs a dual-component system combining a genetically encoded pH biosensor (e.g., pHluorin) with an orthogonal, inducible pH actuator (e.g., a light-gated proton pump or a chemically recruitable proton transporter). This allows for real-time imaging of intracellular pH while simultaneously and precisely manipulating pH in the same cell population. By experimentally clamping or perturbing pH in specific compartments (e.g., cytosol, Golgi) before and during IL-8 induction stimuli, the system directly tests the causal role of pH changes in regulating IL-8 expression and secretion. This move from correlation to causation will define whether pH is a critical signaling node, a downstream homeostatic response, or an epiphenomenon in the inflammatory pathway.

ideas.title: Use proximity labeling with RNA pulldown to map dynamic stability factor interactions under acidic stress
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from chromatin biology, where proximity-labeling enzymes are targeted to specific genomic loci to capture transient protein interactions. The analogous solution uses techniques like ChEC or TurboID fusion proteins to biotinylate proximal proteins for identification. Adapted to the RNA context, we propose fusing an engineered biotin ligase (TurboID/APEX2) to an RNA-binding domain specific for IL-8 mRNA. Expressing this construct in cells under acidic versus normal pH conditions allows for spatially restricted biotinylation of proteins interacting with or near the mRNA. The biotinylated proteins are then captured via streptavidin pulldown and identified by mass spectrometry. This method overcomes limitations of traditional RNA pulldowns by capturing weak, transient, or indirect pH-dependent interactions within the native cellular environment, providing a more comprehensive map of stability factors regulating IL-8 mRNA during acidosis.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal mapping of acidosis-induced transcription factor activation using multiplexed pH biosensors and live-cell imaging
ideas.description: This project integrates genetically encoded pH biosensors with live-cell imaging of transcription factor activity reporters to investigate the role of acidosis in IL-8 regulation. By performing multiplexed imaging in cells subjected to acidic stress, we can simultaneously visualize intracellular pH dynamics and the activation of candidate transcription factors beyond AP-1/NF-kB, such as HIF-1α, ATF, or NFAT. This approach allows us to establish direct causal and temporal relationships between specific pH changes in organelles like the cytoplasm or nucleus and the induction of distinct signaling pathways. The synergy enables the discovery of pH-sensitive transcriptional networks by screening for factors whose activation precisely coincides with biosensor-detected acidification events.

ideas.title: Adapting Electrophoretic Mobility Shift Assay (EMSA) and Supershift Strategies from Immunology to Map pH-Activated Transcription Factor Subunits
ideas.description: This idea adapts a classic molecular virology technique to solve a specific problem in cancer biology. In virology, identifying the specific subunits of transcription factors like NF-κB and AP-1 that are activated during viral infection is routinely accomplished using antibody supershift assays following an Electrophoretic Mobility Shift Assay (EMSA). We propose to directly apply this methodological solution to characterize the exact protein subunits of NF-κB and AP-1 activated by acidic pH in ovarian cancer. By subjecting nuclear extracts from ovarian cancer cells cultured under acidic (tumor microenvironment-like) conditions to EMSA/supershift analysis with a comprehensive antibody panel, we can definitively identify whether acidic pH promotes activation of canonical (e.g., p50-p65) or non-canonical (e.g., p52-RelB) NF-κB dimers, and which Jun/Fos family members comprise the active AP-1 complex, providing mechanistic clarity to the pH-sensing pathway.

ideas.description: This research direction develops and applies novel live-cell compatible chromatin conformation capture techniques, such as live-cell DamID or CRISPR imaging of labeled loci, integrated with FRET biosensors for key acidosis-responsive signaling pathways (e.g., NF-κB, HIF-1α). This allows for the parallel monitoring of pathway activation kinetics and the consequent, dynamic remodeling of the IL-8 promoter's 3D architecture and its long-range enhancer interactions within the same single cell. The synergy creates a 4D (3D space + time) experimental system to determine whether signaling events precede, coincide with, or are a consequence of specific chromatin conformational changes, establishing direct mechanistic causality between the extracellular acidic microenvironment and the gene regulatory structural landscape.

ideas.title: A multi-scale platform to screen cytokine combinations in vitro and validate functional angiogenesis in vivo
ideas.description: This redesigned approach first uses a high-throughput, high-content in vitro co-culture system (e.g., endothelial cells with pericytes in 3D matrices) to screen the six cytokines and their combinations for effects on specific angiogenic steps. The most promising synergistic combinations are then rapidly and quantitatively validated in live transgenic zebrafish embryos, where their impact on functional, perfused vessel formation, remodeling, and stability can be assessed in real time. This integrated platform directly overcomes the fatal flaw of pure in vitro work by ensuring that any identified combinatorial effects are relevant to a living, multicellular system, thereby de-risking translation and providing more physiologically robust data.

ideas.title: Decoding Cytokine-Specific Signaling Logic for Angiogenesis Using a Pathway Interaction Network Model
ideas.description: This research seed idea is expanded via analogy to the problem of mapping complex, interconnected networks in systems biology and metabolic engineering. In that field, predictive models of metabolic flux are built by systematically perturbing pathways and measuring outputs. Adapted to angiogenesis, the research would systematically map intracellular signaling cascades by treating distinct angiogenic functions (e.g., sprouting, lumen formation, maturation) as measurable outputs. The methodology involves combinatorial perturbation of signaling nodes activated by different cytokines in endothelial cells, followed by high-resolution functional phenotyping. The goal is to move beyond a simple one-cytokine-one-pathway map and instead construct a predictive network model that defines the integrative logic—such as pathway synergy, antagonism, or sequential activation—by which cytokine signals are processed to execute specific, discrete steps in blood vessel formation.

ideas.title: Decoupling temporal sequence from concentration in cytokine signaling necessity
ideas.description: This research directly tests if the specific order of cytokine exposure is required for cell fate decisions, independent of confounding concentration effects. It uses engineered inducible signaling systems and microfluidics to deliver cytokines in precise sequences at fixed, saturating concentrations, eliminating dose as a variable. Single-cell analysis then determines if distinct transcriptional and functional outcomes are uniquely driven by temporal sequence. This approach rigorously isolates timing as the sole experimental variable to challenge the sufficiency of cytokine presence alone.

ideas.title: Validate synergistic cytokine combinations on distinct angiogenic steps in a dynamic in vivo context
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the in vitro screening of synergistic/antagonistic cytokine combinations with in vivo validation of step-specific regulation into a single, iterative pipeline. Promising combinations identified in vitro are immediately tested in animal angiogenesis models using intravital imaging and step-specific reporters to confirm their effect on distinct stages (e.g., sprouting vs. maturation) in real time. The in vivo findings then inform refined secondary in vitro screens, creating a feedback loop that rapidly identifies combinations whose synergistic effects are both potent and contextually relevant to the dynamic physiology of a living organism. This approach moves beyond cataloging interactions to functionally deconstruct the temporal logic of combinatorial angiogenic signaling in vivo.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal Signaling Cartography of Angiogenesis via Programmable Cytokine Landscapes
ideas.description: This research direction integrates high-resolution microfluidic gradient generation with live-cell biosensor imaging and targeted pathway interrogation to create a 'signaling cartography' platform. It will expose endothelial cells to precisely controlled, spatially complex cytokine combinations and gradients that mimic in vivo niches, such as those found in tumor microenvironments or healing wounds. Simultaneously, it will employ FRET-based biosensors and multiplexed activity assays to map how distinct signaling pathways (e.g., MAPK, PI3K, STAT) are activated with spatial and temporal specificity in response to the gradient, directly linking cue geometry to migratory, proliferative, and tubulogenic behaviors. This approach moves beyond studying pathways in isolation to decipher how they are integrated and prioritized by cells navigating realistic chemical landscapes.

ideas.title: Context-aware, data-driven ensemble modeling of cytokine networks for patient-stratified perturbation prediction
ideas.description: This research integrates patient-specific multi-omic data with a prior knowledge cytokine network to generate dynamic, personalized computational models. An ensemble machine learning approach infers context-specific interaction strengths and active sub-networks from transcriptomic and proteomic profiles, moving beyond a static topology. The system's perturbation predictions are then rigorously validated using primary immune cell assays from the same donor cohorts, creating a closed loop of computational prediction and experimental verification. This approach aims to produce clinically relevant, patient-stratified forecasts of immunomodulatory outcomes.

ideas.title: Apply High-Throughput Drug Combination Screening Methodology to Angiogenic Cytokine Testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the high-throughput combinatorial screening methodology successfully used in oncology drug discovery to the problem of testing angiogenic molecule combinations. In oncology, automated platforms systematically test thousands of drug combinations across cancer cell lines using matrix-based designs and synergy quantification models. The adapted approach would implement a similar high-throughput system to test all possible pairs and higher-order combinations of the six cytokines on distinct angiogenic steps in vitro. Using automated liquid handling for precise cytokine mixture preparation across concentration gradients, endothelial cell responses (proliferation, migration, tube formation) would be measured. Data would be analyzed using established synergy models (Bliss independence, Loewe additivity) to quantify interaction effects, creating a comprehensive map of cytokine combinatorial interactions and identifying optimal cocktails for specific angiogenic outcomes.

ideas.title: Adapting a Comparative Physiology Framework to Test Step-Specific Regulation Across Endothelial Cell Lineages
ideas.description: This idea adapts the comparative approach from physiology and evolutionary biology to vascular biology. In the analogous field, to test the conservation of a mechanism, a uniform perturbation is applied to diverse systems and responses are systematically compared. Adapted here, the specific experimental condition that defined a 'step' (e.g., a specific stage of angiogenesis) and its regulation in the original endothelial cell model will be applied in parallel to distinct primary endothelial cells: microvascular (e.g., dermal or cardiac) and arterial (e.g., coronary or aortic). Using a standardized panel of functional and molecular assays, the response patterns will be compared to determine if the step-specific regulatory network architecture is conserved across endothelial lineages or has diverged due to local hemodynamic or functional specializations.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Cytokine Response Profiling via Microfluidic Droplet Barcoding
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from microbial ecology and metagenomics—specifically, microfluidic droplet-based single-cell barcoding—to measure heterogeneity in single endothelial cell responses to cytokines. The analogous field solved the problem of analyzing genomic content from individual cells within complex microbial communities by using droplet microfluidics to isolate, lyse, and uniquely barcode each cell's genetic material. The adapted research idea employs this platform to encapsulate single endothelial cells with cytokine-coated stimulus beads and unique molecular identifiers in droplets. Following cytokine stimulation within the droplet, cells are lysed, and their mRNA is barcoded for downstream sequencing, allowing for the parallel, high-throughput profiling of transcriptional response heterogeneity to specific cytokines across thousands of individual endothelial cells, capturing rare response phenotypes and population diversity.

ideas.title: Longitudinal tracking of single-cell cytokine signaling dynamics and phenotypic evolution
ideas.description: This redesigned approach uses a microfluidic cell culture system to expose single endothelial cells to controlled cytokine pulses while simultaneously monitoring them via live-cell microscopy. Genetically encoded fluorescent biosensors report the real-time activity of specific signaling pathways in each cell in response to cytokine stimulation. The same cells are then tracked over subsequent hours to days to correlate these initial dynamic signaling trajectories with later heterogeneous phenotypic outcomes, such as adhesion molecule expression, morphology changes, or proliferation. This temporal integration distinguishes durable cellular programming from transient signaling noise and reveals causal links between dynamic signaling patterns and ultimate functional heterogeneity.

ideas.title: High-Content Microfluidic Chemotaxis Platform for Multiplexed, Spatiotemporal Analysis of Cell Decision-Making
ideas.description: This research integrates microfluidic gradient generators with automated high-content imaging and analysis to create a platform for dissecting the multi-step process of directed cell migration. The microfluidic device provides precise, tunable, and complex spatial-temporal cytokine patterns, while the imaging system continuously tracks and quantifies multiplexed cellular responses—such as receptor clustering, cytoskeletal remodeling, adhesion site turnover, and ultimate migratory trajectory—all within the same assay. This approach moves beyond simply measuring endpoint migration speed or direction to instead capture the complete cascade of signaling and morphological events that constitute a cell's 'decision' to migrate. Consequently, it allows for the systematic deconstruction of how gradient shape, steepness, and dynamics instruct specific stages of the chemotactic process.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal mapping of acidosis-induced kinase signaling dynamics via high-content phosphoproteomic imaging
ideas.description: This project integrates high-content, multiplexed imaging with phosphoproteomic data to create a spatially resolved map of acidosis signaling. First, global phosphoproteomics under acidic pH identifies key activated kinases and their substrates. These targets then inform the design of a multiplexed imaging assay using fluorescent biosensors or antibodies to simultaneously track the activity and localization of these kinases in single live or fixed cells. The automated image analysis quantifies not just the magnitude of signaling, but also its subcellular compartmentalization, temporal progression, and correlation with phenotypic outcomes like cytoskeletal remodeling or cell death, providing a systems-level view of acidosis response with single-cell spatial resolution.

ideas.title: Apply microfluidic gradient generators with feedback control for dynamic cytokine landscapes
ideas.description: This idea expands the original microfluidics concept by drawing an analogy from chemical process engineering. The analogous problem of creating and maintaining precise concentration gradients in reactor systems is solved using networks of interconnected chambers (like CSTRs in series) with sensor-based feedback control. Adapting this solution, the research proposes a microfluidic device designed as a network of cell culture chambers with controlled interconnections, enabling the generation of stable, spatially complex cytokine gradients. Crucially, it integrates real-time biosensors and a feedback control system to monitor and dynamically adjust the cytokine inputs. This adaptation allows biologists to move beyond simple, static linear gradients and instead create and maintain complex, predefined spatial cytokine patterns, or even programmatically evolve the gradient landscape over time, mimicking dynamic in vivo scenarios like an evolving inflammatory site, to study directional migration and decision-making in immune cells with unprecedented physiological relevance.

ideas.title: Apply Hi-C to map acidosis-induced structural changes in the IL-8 genomic neighborhood, inspired by urban planning network analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from urban planning network analysis to study genomic architecture. In urban planning, researchers quantify how physical changes to a city's layout alter connectivity and flow between districts using network graphs. Analogously, we propose applying high-resolution Hi-C to generate genome-wide chromatin interaction maps in cells under normal physiological pH versus acidosis. By analyzing these maps as 3D genomic networks, we can quantify acidosis-induced changes in the IL-8 promoter's role as a connectivity hub and identify specific long-range interactions with enhancers or other regulatory elements that are strengthened or weakened, treating these altered interaction frequencies as analogous to changed traffic flows in an urban network following a perturbation.

ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from plant abiotic stress research, where phosphoproteomics combined with kinase activity inference was used to map drought signaling. The analogous solution involved using phosphoproteomic data not just to identify phosphorylation changes, but to computationally infer the upstream kinases responsible via methods like kinase-substrate enrichment analysis (KSEA) and phosphosite motif analysis. Adapted to the original research context, the method involves performing global phosphoproteomic analysis on cells subjected to acidic pH, then applying these computational inference techniques to predict and map the specific upstream kinases (e.g., identifying whether p38 MAPK, JNK, or specific CAMK family members are activated) that form the signaling network responding to acidosis, thereby providing a mechanistic, kinase-centric map of the acidosis response.

ideas.title: Test for mechanistic priming between angiogenic steps using sequential, orthogonal perturbation
ideas.description: This study aims to dissect true interdependence from mere sequentiality in angiogenesis. An in vitro system uses endothelial cells where a key effector for a later step (e.g., a small GTPase for lumenogenesis) is placed under control of an orthogonal, inducible system (e.g., a chemically dimerizable construct). Cells are first allowed to complete an early step (e.g., VEGF-induced migration and contact), and then the later-step effector is directly activated or inhibited without further affecting the early step machinery. If tube formation proceeds normally or is aberrant despite correct cellular positioning, it suggests independence; if it is enhanced or requires specific priming from the migration step, it demonstrates direct mechanistic coupling.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismometer Array Principles for Multi-Pathway FRET Imaging
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where arrays of synchronized seismometers provide a dynamic, system-wide view of subsurface activity, to the context of live-cell biosensing. The analogous seismological solution uses sensor networks to distinguish and track different wave types from a single event in real time. Adapted to cell biology, this involves engineering a multiplexed array of spectrally distinct FRET biosensors, each targeting a different component (e.g., receptor, kinase, second messenger, transcription factor) within a connected signaling network. By co-expressing this suite of orthogonal biosensors in single cells and employing multiplexed imaging, researchers can monitor the activation dynamics of multiple pathways simultaneously. This approach moves beyond observing one pathway in isolation to reconstructing the live, causal signaling 'topography' of a cell, revealing the sequence of events, branching points, and feedback loops that define complex cellular responses to stimuli.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography for Spatiotemporal pH Mapping in Cellular Microenvironments
ideas.description: This idea adapts the computational inversion methods of geophysical seismic tomography to the problem of visualizing intracellular pH dynamics. In seismology, waves from multiple events are used to model subsurface structures. Here, a suite of spectrally distinct, genetically encoded pH biosensors targeted to various organelles (e.g., Golgi, endosomes, cytoplasm) will serve as the internal 'sensor array'. During IL-8 induction, high-content, time-lapse imaging will capture the fluorescence changes from this sensor panel. Advanced computational inversion algorithms, inspired by tomographic reconstruction, will then analyze this multi-parameter data to generate a dynamic, 3D-plus-time model of pH changes, revealing how pH perturbations initiate, propagate, and resolve within the cellular microenvironment in response to inflammatory signaling.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporally Precise Optical Control of Single-Step Angiogenic Dynamics In Vivo
ideas.description: This project overcomes the ambiguity of systemic interventions by developing a method to manipulate and observe a single angiogenic step with cellular precision in a living animal. It employs a transgenic mouse model expressing a photoactivatable Rho GTPase actuator within endothelial cells, combined with chronic cranial or dorsal skinfold window chambers for stable imaging. Using targeted pulses of light, specific cytoskeletal behaviors (e.g., lamellipodia formation) are triggered in individual endothelial cells at the vascular front during physiological or pathological angiogenesis. The direct, immediate consequences on cell shape, migration, stalk/tip cell specification, and nascent sprout morphology are quantified via multiphoton microscopy, providing causal evidence for step-specific regulation within the intact tissue microenvironment.

ideas.title: Causal step validation via integrated microfluidic perturbation and high-content imaging
ideas.description: This system combines multiplexed high-content imaging with an on-chip microfluidic network capable of delivering precise, timed perturbations to live cells or samples within the imaging field. By programmatically introducing inhibitors, activators, or physical changes at specific time points, the platform moves beyond correlation to actively test the necessity and order of proposed biochemical or phenotypic steps. Automated image analysis then quantifies the differential outcomes of these interventions, generating causal maps of multi-step processes rather than just simultaneous measurements. This integrated approach transforms image-based assays from observational tools into platforms for experimental validation of mechanistic pathways.

ideas.title: Relaxing angiogenic step independence via coupled oscillator models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that angiogenic steps are independent by drawing an analogy from systems neuroscience, where the solution to understanding coordinated locomotion involved modeling independent limb controllers as coupled oscillators. The analogous solution showed that modulating one oscillator directly alters others via coupling. Adapted to angiogenesis, this means developing a coupled dynamical systems model where steps like migration and tube formation are represented as interacting oscillatory or state processes. The research would test this by applying precise perturbations to one step (e.g., via a tunable inhibitor of endothelial cell migration) and quantitatively measuring not just that step's output, but also how the timing, rate, and success of subsequent tube formation is predictably altered, providing evidence for direct dynamical coupling rather than sequential independence.

ideas.title: Adapting Satellite Constellation Monitoring for Multiplexed Step Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Earth observation and remote sensing to the original problem of using high-content imaging for multiplexed step analysis. In remote sensing, automated pipelines analyze multi-spectral data from satellite constellations to monitor and quantify multiple, simultaneous environmental processes across a wide area. By analogy, we treat a high-content microscopy image as a complex 'cellular landscape.' The adapted research idea is to develop an automated image analysis pipeline that segments this landscape into individual cells or regions of interest. It then uses the multiplexed fluorescent channels (analogous to spectral bands) as distinct signatures to simultaneously detect, classify, and quantify the progression of multiple biological steps or states within each entity in a single, high-content assay, enabling a systems-level view of coordinated cellular processes.
